Ayahs

ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ (١)
The All-beneficent (1)
عَلَّمَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ (٢)
has taught the Quran. (2)
خَلَقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ (٣)
He created man, (3)
عَلَّمَهُ ٱلْبَيَانَ (٤)
[and] taught him articulate speech. (4)
ٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ بِحُسْبَانٍ (٥)
The sun and the moon are [disposed] calculatedly, (5)
وَٱلنَّجْمُ وَٱلشَّجَرُ يَسْجُدَانِ (٦)
and the herb and the tree prostrate [to Allah]. (6)

Content of Surah Ar-Rahman

This Surah generally describes the various "spiritual" and "material" blessings of Allah that He has bestowed upon His servants and immersed them within. So much so, that this Surah could be named the "Surah of Mercy" or the "Surah of Blessing." For this reason, it begins with the blessed name "Ar-Rahman" (The Most Merciful), which reflects the "all-encompassing Divine Mercy," and concludes with the majesty and honor of Allah. The verse "فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ‌" (So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?), through which He seeks acknowledgment from His servants regarding His blessings, is repeated 31 times.

Therefore, from one perspective, the entire Surah is a cohesive section centered on the blessings of the Gracious Allah. From another perspective, its content can be divided into several parts:

The first part, which serves as the introduction and beginning of the Surah, speaks of the great blessings of creation, education and training, accounting and the balance, the means of human welfare, and his spiritual and physical sustenance.

The second part is an explanation regarding the nature of the creation of mankind and Jinn.

The third part describes the signs and verses of Allah in the earth and the heavens.

In the fourth part, it goes beyond worldly blessings to speak of the blessings of the Hereafter. With particular precision and elegance, the details of Paradise's blessings—including gardens, springs, fruits, beautiful and faithful spouses, and various types of garments—are explained.

And finally, in the fifth part of this Surah, there is a brief reference to the fate of criminals and some of their painful punishments. However, since the foundation of the Surah is based on the expression of Divine Mercy, this section is not explained in great detail. In contrast, the blessings of Paradise are mentioned elaborately and extensively, such that they drown the hearts of the believers in joy and hope, remove the dust of sorrow from the heart, and plant the seedling of longing in the soul.

The repetition of the verse "فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ‌" at short intervals gives the Surah a beautiful and interesting rhythm. When combined with its beautiful content, it acquires a stunning attraction. Thus, it is no surprise that in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated that he said:

"Everything has a bride, and the bride of the Quran is Surah Ar-Rahman, may His mention be exalted." [^1]

It is noteworthy that although the word "Arus" (bride) in the Persian language refers specifically to a woman, in Arabic culture, it is applied to both the man and the woman as long as they are in the wedding ceremony. [^2] Since the man and woman in such a ceremony are in their best and most beautiful states and receive the most complete honors, this word is applied to very beautiful and cherished entities.

The reason for choosing the name "Ar-Rahman" for this Surah and its suitability is self-evident.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, beginning of Surah Ar-Rahman. This Hadith is also mentioned in Ad-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 140. [^2]: Lisan al-Arab, Majma' al-Bahrayn, Sihah al-Lughah, etc.

The Virtue of Reciting Surah Ar-Rahman

Since this Surah awakens the sense of gratitude in humans in the most sublime way, and the description of material and spiritual blessings of this world and the hereafter increases the longing for obedience and servitude in the heart, many virtues for its recitation have been mentioned in narrations. Of course, this refers to a recitation that penetrates the depths of the human soul and becomes a source of action, not merely a movement of the tongue.

Among these, we read in a Hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Whoever recites Surah Ar-Rahman, Allah will have mercy on his weakness (in fulfilling the gratitude for blessings) and He Himself will fulfill the right of gratitude for the blessings He has bestowed upon him." [^1]

In another Hadith in Thawab al-A'mal from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Never abandon the recitation of Surah Ar-Rahman and standing by it, for this Surah never settles in the hearts of hypocrites. On the Day of Resurrection, Allah will place it in the form of a human who has the most beautiful face and the most fragrant scent, then it will stand in a place closest to Allah Almighty among all! Allah will ask this Surah: 'Who stood by your content in the life of the world and continued your recitation?' It will reply: 'O Lord! So-and-so and so-and-so.' At that moment, their faces will turn white. Allah will say to them: 'Now intercede for whomever you wish.' They will intercede as much as they desire, and they will not intercede for anyone except that it will be said to them: 'Enter Paradise and dwell wherever you wish!'" [^1]

And in another Hadith from him عليه السلام, it is stated:

"Whoever recites Surah Ar-Rahman and says at every 'فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ‌': La bi-shay'in min ala'ika Rabbi akdhib ('O Lord, I do not deny any of Your favors'), if he recites it at night and then dies, he will die a martyr; and if he recites it during the day and dies, he will also die a martyr!" [^2]

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 187. [^1] and [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 92, p. 306.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ الرَّحْمنُ (1) عَلَّمَ الْقُرْآنَ (2) خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ (3) عَلَّمَهُ الْبَيانَ (4) الشَّمْسُ وَ الْقَمَرُ بِحُسْبانٍ (5) وَ النَّجْمُ وَ الشَّجَرُ يَسْجُدانِ (6)

Commentary

The Beginning of Divine Blessings

Since this Surah—as we have said—expresses various types of great Divine blessings and favors, it begins with the holy name "Ar-Rahman," which is a symbol of His all-encompassing mercy. For if it were not for His attribute of "Rahmaniyyah" (All-Mercifulness), He would not have spread such a banquet of blessings for both friend and foe.

Thus, He says: "The Most Merciful (Allah)" (الرَّحْمنُ) [^1]

"Has taught the Quran" (عَلَّمَ الْقُرْآنَ).

In this way, He mentions the first and most important blessing as the "teaching of the Quran." What an interesting and profound expression! For if we reflect correctly, this Glorious Quran is the source of all favors, the means of attaining every blessing, and the way to benefit from all spiritual and material favors. Interestingly, He mentions the blessing of "teaching the Quran" even before the issue of the "creation of man" and "teaching of speech," whereas in natural order, the creation of man should be mentioned first, then the blessing of teaching speech, and then the blessing of teaching the Quran. However, the greatness of the Quran necessitated that, contrary to this natural order, it be spoken of first.

This verse also serves as a response to the Arab polytheists who, when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله mentioned the name "Ar-Rahman" and invited them to prostrate to the Most Merciful Allah, said as an excuse: "And what is Ar-Rahman?" (Al-Furqan: 60).

The Quran says: The Most Merciful is He who taught the Quran, created man, and taught him speech.

In any case, the name "Ar-Rahman" has the broadest concept among the names of the Lord after the name "Allah." For we know that Allah possesses two types of mercy: "General Mercy" and "Specific Mercy." The name "Ar-Rahman" refers to His "General Mercy" which encompasses everyone, and the name "Ar-Rahim" refers to His "Specific Mercy" which is exclusive to the people of faith and obedience. Perhaps for this reason, the name "Ar-Rahman" is never applied to anyone other than Allah (unless accompanied by the word "Abd"), but the description "Rahim" is said of others as well. This is because no one possesses general mercy except Him, while specific mercy exists, albeit in a weak form, among humans and other creatures.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Ar-Rahman is a specific name with a general attribute, and Ar-Rahim is a general name with a specific attribute." (Meaning it is a name exclusive to God whose mercy encompasses everyone, while Rahim is a name used for both God and creation—as the Quran describes the Prophet of Islam as Ra'uf Rahim (At-Tawbah: 128)—but its concept is a specific and defined mercy).

Regarding whom Allah taught the Quran to, commentators have mentioned various interpretations: sometimes they say to Gabriel and the angels, sometimes to the person of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and sometimes to all humans and even Jinn.

Since this Surah expresses Divine mercies to Jinn and mankind, and therefore asks them 31 times after mentioning these blessings, "Which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?", the last interpretation is the most suitable: that Allah taught this Quran to Jinn and mankind through His great Prophet Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله. [^1]

After mentioning the peerless blessing of the Quran, He proceeds to the most important blessing in the subsequent sequence, saying: "He created man" (خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ). Certainly, "man" here refers to the human species, not Prophet "Adam," because He speaks of him separately a few verses later; nor does it refer only to the person of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, although he is the most superior and sublime instance of it.

The vastness of the blessing of "speech" (Bayan) that follows is another evidence for the generality of the meaning of "man." Therefore, other interpretations besides what has been said do not seem correct.

Truly, the creation of man—this wondrous collection of the universe, this summary of beings, this "microcosm" in which the "macrocosm" is inscribed—is a unique and peerless blessing, for every dimension of his existence is itself a great blessing.

Although the beginning of his existence was nothing more than an insignificant drop of fluid, or more accurately, a microscopic being swimming in that fluid, under the Lordship of the Creator, he undergoes such stages of perfection that he rises to the rank of the most noble being in creation.

Mentioning the name of man after the Quran is also noteworthy, for the Quran is the collection of the secrets of existence in written form, and man is the summary of these secrets in existential form, and each is a copy of this great and vast universe!

The next verse points to one of the most important blessings after the blessing of human creation, adding: "He taught him speech" (عَلَّمَهُ الْبَيانَ). "Bayan" (speech/expression) has a broad linguistic meaning and refers to anything that clarifies and reveals something. Therefore, it includes not only speech and talk but also writing, calligraphy, and various types of intellectual and logical reasoning that clarify different and complex issues—all are gathered within the concept of Bayan, though the hallmark of this collection is "speaking."

Although we consider speaking a simple matter due to habit, in reality, speaking is one of the most complex and delicate human actions; rather, it can be said that no task is as delicate and complex! For on one hand, the vocal apparatuses cooperate to create different sounds: the lungs collect air and gradually send it out through the larynx, the vocal cords vibrate, and completely different sounds—some indicating satisfaction, others anger, others a plea for help, others speech, and others love or enmity—are created. Then these sounds, with the help of the tongue, lips, teeth, and the oral cavity, produce the letters of the alphabet with specific speed and delicacy. In other words, that continuous and monotonous sound coming out of the larynx is cut into various shapes and sizes, and letters are formed from it.

On the other hand, there is the issue of coining words, where man, due to intellectual progress, coins various words for his different material and spiritual needs. It is amazing that there is no limitation for man in coining words, and the number of languages existing in the world is so large that they cannot be precisely enumerated, and even with the passage of time, new words and languages are gradually formed.

Some consider the number of existing languages in the world to be three thousand! Others have mentioned a higher number. [^1] But it seems the goal was to count the primary languages; otherwise, if we consider local dialects, the matter would go even further, to the point where it is sometimes seen that two adjacent villages speak two different local dialects.

On the third hand, there is the issue of organizing sentence structures and arguments or expressing feelings through the intellect and thought, which is the soul of expression and speech. For this reason, speaking is exclusive to humans.

It is true that many animals produce various sounds to communicate their needs, but the number of these sounds is very limited, vague, and ambiguous, whereas expression is available to humans in a vast and unlimited way because Allah has granted them the necessary intellectual power for speaking.

Beyond these, if we consider the role of "Bayan" in the evolution and progress of human life and the emergence and advancement of civilizations, we will be certain that if it were not for this great blessing, man could never have easily transferred his experiences and sciences from one generation to another, causing the progress of science, knowledge, civilization, religion, and ethics. If one day this great blessing were taken away from humans, human society would rapidly regress. Whenever we interpret Bayan in its broad sense—which includes writing, calligraphy, and even various arts—its exceptionally important role in human life becomes clearer.

This is where we understand why, after the blessing of human creation, the teaching of speech is mentioned in this Surah Ar-Rahman, which is a collection of the Lord's favors.

Then He moves to the fourth great blessing among the favors of the Most Merciful Allah, saying: "The sun and the moon [move] according to a precise calculation" (الشَّمْسُ وَ الْقَمَرُ بِحُسْبانٍ). [^1] The very existence of the sun is among the greatest blessings for man, for without the light and heat resulting from it, life in the solar system would be impossible. As previously stated, every movement and motion that takes place on our earthly sphere has its primary source in the light and heat of the sun. The growth and development of plants and all food materials, the falling of rain, the blowing of winds—all are by the grace of this Divine gift. The moon also plays an important role in human life; besides being a lamp for his dark nights, its gravity—which is the source of tides in the oceans—is a factor for the preservation of life in the seas and for irrigating many coasts where rivers flow into the sea.

In addition to all this, the fixed system of the movement of these two (or in other words, the movement of the moon around the earth and the movement of the earth around the sun), which causes the regular occurrence of day and night, the year, the month, and the different seasons, is the cause of order in human life and planning for commercial, industrial, and agricultural affairs. If it were not for this regular course, human life would never have found order.

Not only do the movements of these celestial bodies have a very precise system, but their mass, gravity, and the distance they have from the earth and from each other are all based on calculation and "Husban." Certainly, if any of these matters were disrupted, massive disturbances would occur in the solar system and consequently in the system of human life.

It is amazing that when the components of this system were separated from the sun, they appeared very scattered and disordered, but finally stabilized in their current form. One natural scientist says in this regard: "Our solar system was formed from a (seemingly) chaotic mixture of materials and components that separated from the sun at a temperature of 12,000 degrees and scattered into infinite space at an unimaginable speed. However, in this very apparent chaos and atmospheric revolution, such a precise and regular order was created that today we can even predict the minutes and moments of its future events. The result of this system and order is that our celestial conditions have remained the same for a thousand million years." [^1] It is also noteworthy that although the sun apparently remains fixed in the center of the solar system, it must not be forgotten that it, along with all its planets and moons, is moving within the galaxy to which it belongs toward a specific point (the famous star Vega), and this movement also has a specific order and speed.

[^1]: The Secret of Human Creation, p. 28.

In the fifth great favor, He looks from the heaven to the earth and says: "And the star and the tree prostrate [to Him]" (وَ النَّجْمُ وَ الشَّجَرُ يَسْجُدانِ).

"Najm" sometimes means a star, and sometimes it means plants without a stem. Here, by the context of "Shajar" (tree), the second meaning—plants without a stem—is intended. [^1] Originally, this word means "to rise" or "to appear." If these plants are called "Najm," it is because they emerge from the earth, and if a star is called "Najm," it is also because of its rising.

We know that all human food materials are originally taken from plants, with the difference that man consumes some directly, and another part is used to feed animals that are part of human food. This meaning is true even for sea animals, for they also feed on very small plants, millions of which grow in every corner of the sea under the sunlight and move within the waves.

In this way, "Najm" includes all types of small and creeping plants (like squash, cucumber, and the like), and "Shajar" includes all types of stemmed plants like grains, fruit trees, and others.

The expression "Yasjudan" (these two prostrate) refers to their unconditional submission to the laws of creation and their path toward human benefits—a path that Allah has determined for them and which they traverse without deficiency.

It also points to their monotheistic secrets, for in every leaf and every seed of a plant, there are wondrous signs of the greatness and knowledge of the Lord, and "every leaf is a ledger of the Creator's knowledge!" [^1] Of course, the possibility has also been suggested that "Najm" in the above verse refers to the stars of the sky, but what we said above is more suitable and consistent with the existing context in the verse.

Key Point: A Look at Several Narrations

In Islamic sources, narrations have been cited regarding the above verses which are of the nature of general interpretation through clear examples, and each explains a part of the verses, including:

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that in the interpretation of "taught him speech," he said: "Speech is the Greatest Name (Ism al-A'zam) by which everything is known." [^2] We had a discussion about the Greatest Name and its interpretation in Vol. 7, p. 30 (under verse 180 of Surah Al-A'raf).

In another Hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, it is stated that the meaning of "The Most Merciful taught the Quran" is that Allah taught the Quran to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the meaning of "created man" is the creation of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, and "taught him speech" is the explanation of all matters that people need.

It is clear that the above narrations do not limit the generality of the concepts of these verses, although they guide us to their prominent instances.

[^1]: Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "An-Najm is that which has no stem among plants." [^1]: Regarding the meaning of "the prostration of various beings in the universe," we have discussed it extensively in Vol. 14, p. 48 onwards (under verse 18 of Surah Al-Hajj), and also in Vol. 12, p. 133 onwards (under verse 44 of Surah Al-Isra). [^2]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 197.

Ayahs

وَٱلسَّمَآءَ رَفَعَهَا وَوَضَعَ ٱلْمِيزَانَ (٧)
He raised the heaven high and set up the balance, (7)
أَلَّا تَطْغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْمِيزَانِ (٨)
declaring, ‘Do not infringe the balance! (8)
وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلْوَزْنَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ وَلَا تُخْسِرُوا۟ ٱلْمِيزَانَ (٩)
Maintain the weights with justice, and do not shorten the balance!’ (9)
وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَضَعَهَا لِلْأَنَامِ (١٠)
And the earth—He laid it out for mankind. (10)
فِيهَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ وَٱلنَّخْلُ ذَاتُ ٱلْأَكْمَامِ (١١)
In it are fruits and date-palms with sheaths, (11)
وَٱلْحَبُّ ذُو ٱلْعَصْفِ وَٱلرَّيْحَانُ (١٢)
grain with husk, and fragrant herbs. (12)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (١٣)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (13)

Commentary

He Raised the Heaven and Set a Balance for Everything

These verses continue the exposition of blessings mentioned in the preceding verses. As we noted, those verses referred to five great blessings among the most significant Divine favors. Thus, the first verse under discussion points to the sixth blessing—the creation of the heaven—stating: "And the heaven He raised high" (وَ السَّماءَ رَفَعَها).

Whether "heaven" in this verse refers to the "upward direction," the "celestial bodies," or the "Earth's atmosphere" (the vast layer of air surrounding this globe that acts as a shield protecting the Earth from harmful cosmic rays and meteorites, and retains the sun's heat and moisture rising from the seas to form clouds and rain), each is a magnificent gift and an unparalleled blessing. Without it, human life would be impossible or severely deficient.

Indeed, light and illumination—the sources of warmth, guidance, life, and motion—come from the direction of the heaven; rain falls from the heaven; and the descent of revelation is also from the heaven. (In this sense, "heaven" encompasses both material and spiritual concepts).

Beyond all this, this uplifted heaven, in all its meanings, is a great sign among the signs of Allah, providing the best assistance to humanity on the path of knowing Him. When the "people of understanding" (Ulul-Albab) contemplate it, they involuntarily exclaim: رَبَّنا ما خَلَقْتَ هذا باطِلًا: "Our Lord, You did not create this aimlessly" (Al-Imran: 191).

Then, He moves to the seventh blessing, stating: "And He set the balance" (وَ وَضَعَ الْمِيزانَ).

"Mizan" (Balance) refers to any means of measurement: the measurement of truth from falsehood, justice from oppression, values, and the rights of humans in various social stages and paths. "Mizan" includes every cosmic law and legislative decree, as they are all means of measurement.

While it is true that "Mizan" literally means a scale or a tool for weighing the mass of objects, the "Mizan" mentioned in this verse after the creation of the heaven certainly carries a broad concept encompassing every means of measurement and all laws of creation and legislation, not just a tool for weighing goods.

Fundamentally, the elevation of the heaven and the precise order governing millions of celestial spheres would be impossible without a "balance" and calculated laws. The fact that some interpretations define "Mizan" as the "Quran," "Justice," "Sharia," or "Scales" is because each refers to an instance of this universal and expansive concept.

In the following verse, He draws a striking conclusion from this subject, adding: "The purpose of placing the balance in the universe is so that you too observe the balance and do not transgress therein" (أَلَّا تَطْغَوْا فِي الْمِيزانِ). You are also a part of this vast universe and can never live as a discordant patch in this great world. The universe has an order and a balance; you too must have an order and a balance.

If balance and law were removed from the macro-universe, it would head toward annihilation; similarly, if your life lacks order and balance, you will head toward destruction.

What a beautiful transition from the entire universe to the human being, harmonizing the laws governing the "Macrocosm" (the universe) with the laws governing the life of the human "Microcosm." This is the reality of Monotheism (Tawhid)—that the governing principles are the same everywhere.

Once again, He emphasizes the issue of justice and weighing, saying: "And establish weight with justice and do not fall short in the balance" (وَ أَقِيمُوا الْوزْنَ بِالْقِسْطِ وَ لا تُخْسِرُوا الْمِيزانَ).

It is noteworthy that "Mizan" is mentioned three times in these verses, even though pronouns could have been used in the second and third instances. This indicates that "Mizan" in these three verses carries three different nuances that a pronoun could not convey, as necessitated by the context: 1. In the first instance, it refers to the balance, criteria, and laws Allah has placed throughout the entire universe. 2. In the second instance, it refers to humans not transgressing in all balances of individual and social life, which is naturally a narrower scope. 3. In the third instance, it emphasizes "weight" in its specific sense, commanding that one should not cheat or fall short when weighing items during trade—an even more specific scope.

Thus, a beautiful cohesion is employed in the verses as a hierarchy regarding the issue of balance and measurement, moving from the largest circles to the smaller ones. [^1] The importance of the "balance" in any sense for human life is such that if even its most limited instance—the "scale"—were removed for a single day, we would be plagued by immense trouble, chaos, and disputes in the exchange of goods. Proportionately, if its broader concepts were removed, the disorder would be even greater and beyond calculation.

From what has been said, it becomes clear why some narrations interpret "Mizan" as the existence of the "Imam" (عليه السلام); it is because the blessed existence of the Infallible Imam is a means for measuring truth from falsehood, a criterion for discerning realities, and an effective factor for guidance. [^2] Similarly, interpreting "Mizan" as the "Holy Quran" points to the same meaning.

In any case, considering that all these verses are in the context of mentioning Divine blessings, it is clear that the existence of the "balance"—whether in the entire universe, in human society and social relations, or in labor and trade—is among the precious blessings of Allah.

[^1]: Fakhr al-Razi says in his commentary: "Mizan" in the first verse is a noun of instrument (meaning the tool of measurement), in the second verse it has an infinitive meaning (the act of weighing), and in the third verse it has a passive meaning, referring to the "object being weighed." [^2]: This Hadith is narrated in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام). It is a lengthy Hadith; we have only quoted the essence of a portion of it (Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim, Vol. 2, p. 343).

Then, He descends from the heaven to the earth and says: "And the earth He laid out for the creatures" (وَ الْأَرْضَ وَضَعَها لِلْأَنامِ).

Some have interpreted "Anam" as "humans," some as "humans and jinn," and others as "every living being." While a group of linguists and commentators have also interpreted it as "creation" in general, the existing context and the addresses of the Surah directed at both humans and jinn indicate that it refers to both groups here.

Indeed, this terrestrial globe, mentioned here as a significant Divine gift and referred to in other verses as "Mihad" (a cradle), is a secure, calm, and prepared dwelling. Most of us do not realize its importance in normal circumstances, but when a small earthquake disrupts everything, or a volcano buries a city under molten lava and smoke, we realize what a blessing and grace this stable earth is. This becomes even clearer if we contemplate what scientists have said about the speed of the Earth's rotation around itself and its orbit around the sun; the importance of this tranquility despite its dizzying speed—not just one movement, but several types—becomes more evident. [^1]

The use of the term "Wada'a" (laid out/placed) for the earth in contrast to "Rafa'a" (raised) for the heaven, besides the specific literary beauty of this contrast, is a meaningful allusion to the earth and its resources being submissive to man. As we read in Surah Al-Mulk, verse 15: هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ ذَلُولًا فَامْشُوا فِي مَناكِبِها وَ كُلُوا مِنْ رِزْقِهِ: "It is He who made the earth 'submissive' and 'tame' for you, so walk among its slopes and eat of His provision."

Thus, He identifies the eighth great blessing in this series.

In the next verse, He turns to the ninth and tenth blessings, which constitute part of human food sources, saying: "Therein are fruits and date-palms with sheaths" (فِيها فاكِهَةٌ وَ النَّخْلُ ذاتُ الْأَكْمامِ).

"Fakihah" refers to any kind of fruit (as Raghib stated in Al-Mufradat). There is no reason for some to interpret it as all fruits except dates, other than the fact that "date-palms" (Nakhl) are mentioned separately in the verse. This repetition is likely due to the great importance of the date-palm and dates. (We have provided a detailed discussion on the nutritional benefits of dates and their various life-sustaining substances under verse 11 of Surah Al-Nahl and verse 25 of Surah Maryam). [^1]

"Akmam" is the plural of "Kumm" (rhyming with 'jinn'), referring to the sheath that covers the fruit. "Kumm" (rhyming with 'qum') means a sleeve that covers the arm, and "Kummah" (rhyming with 'qubbah') means a cap that covers the head. [^2] The choice of this description for the fruit of the date-palm—which is initially hidden in a sheath, then the sheath bursts and the cluster of dates emerges beautifully—may be due to its striking beauty, or because of the benefits hidden within that sheath, which contains a specific essence used for both food and medicine. Above all, this sheath, like a mother's womb, nurtures the offspring of the palm for a time, protecting them from pests. Once they are able to continue their path in the air and sunlight, the sheath separates and falls away.

Furthermore, the specific state of this tree's fruit—being first in a sheath and then emerging as a cluster—makes harvesting it easy. If the tall date-palm had scattered fruits like an apple tree, harvesting would be extremely difficult.

Finally, He speaks of the eleventh and twelfth favors as follows: "And grain having husks and scented plants" (وَ الْحَبُّ ذُو الْعَصْفِ وَ الرَّيْحانُ).

Grains are food for humans, and their fresh or dry leaves (husks/straw) are food for the animals that serve humans, providing milk, meat, skin, and wool. Thus, nothing from it is useless or meant to be discarded.

On the other hand, He created fragrant plants and flowers on the earth to scent the physical and spiritual senses and grant the soul peace and vitality. In this way, He has completed His blessings upon man.

"Habb" refers to any kind of grain. "Asf" refers to the leaves and parts that separate from plants and are sometimes scattered by the wind; it is also used to mean "straw." Many meanings have been given for "Rayhan," including fragrant plants and any kind of provision; here, the first meaning is most appropriate.

After mentioning these various material and spiritual blessings, in the final verse under discussion, He addresses both jinn and humans, saying: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you two deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

Blessings, each more precious and valuable than the last, blessings that encompass your entire life, each a clear sign of the power, grace, and mercy of the Lord—how is it possible to deny these?! This is a rhetorical question (Istifham Taqriari) used to elicit an admission. Therefore, in the narration we read at the beginning of the Surah, we are commanded to say after this sentence: "O Lord! We do not deny any of Your favors" (لا بشى‌ء من آلائك رب اكذب).

Although the previous verses only mentioned humans and did not explicitly mention the jinn, the subsequent verses show that the dual pronoun (kuma - "you two") addresses these two groups.

In any case, Allah places humans and jinn before this reality to contemplate these matters. Then, without needing further instruction, they should ask their own intellect: "Is any of these Divine blessings deniable?" If not, why do they not recognize their Benefactor? Why do they not use the gratitude for the Benefactor as a means to know Him? And why do they not bow their heads in submission at His sacred threshold?! The expression "Which" (ayyi) indicates that every single one of these blessings is a proof of the Lordship of God and His grace and kindness, let alone the sum of them.

Key Points

1. Recognizing Blessings is the Ladder to Knowing Allah

A little reflection on the twelve blessings mentioned above (the Quran, the creation of man, the teaching of speech, the organized calculation of time, the creation of plants and various trees, the creation of the heaven, the governance of laws, the creation of the earth with its features, the creation of fruits, the creation of the date-palm, the creation of grains, and the creation of flowers and fragrant plants), with the details, subtleties, and secrets hidden in each, is enough to stir the sense of gratitude in man and send him in search of the Gnosis and recognition of the Source of these blessings.

For this reason, after mentioning these blessings, Allah Almighty takes an admission from His servants regarding each one, repeating this sentence 31 times throughout the Surah after mentioning other blessings.

This repetition not only does not contradict eloquence but is itself a technique of eloquence. It is exactly like a father addressing his forgetful son, saying: "Did you forget you were a small and helpless child? What hardships I endured to raise you? Did you forget you fell ill and I brought the best doctors for you? What troubles I took for your medicine and treatment? Did you forget when you reached youth and needed a spouse, I chose the purest of spouses for you? Did you forget when you needed a home and life's necessities, I provided everything for you? So where does this rebellion, transgression, unkindness, and disloyalty come from?"

The Gracious Allah also reminds this forgetful human of His various blessings, and after each section of these blessings, He asks him: "Which of these can you deny?" So where does this disobedience and rebellion come from? Especially since obeying Me is the secret to your own perfection and progress, and nothing from it benefits your Lord.

2. The Issue of Order and Calculation in Life

In our bodies, more than twenty metals and metalloids are used, each with a specific quality and a certain quantity. Whenever the slightest change occurs in their balance and measure, our health is endangered. For example, in the hot season when a person sweats a lot, they may suffer from heatstroke; without any other illness, they might reach the brink of death. The cause is a very simple matter: the reduction of body water and blood salt. The treatment is nothing but drinking water and consuming extra salt!

This is a simple example of order and calculation in the structure of our bodies. Sometimes the measurements in the structure of more delicate entities like cells, and even more delicate ones like atoms, are so precise that a thousandth or even a millionth of a millimeter or milligram is decisive—to the point that scientists are forced to use electronic brains for these precise calculations.

This is in the cosmic system (Takwin). In social currents as well, deviation from the law of justice often swallows a nation into the abyss of non-existence.

Fourteen centuries ago, the Holy Quran unveiled this reality with the expressions we read in the above verses. With the sentence "And the heaven He raised high and He set the balance, so that you do not transgress in the balance," it has said all that needs to be said. It has equated transgression and defiance of legislative laws with transgression and defiance of the cosmic laws governing the heavens.

What a magnificent image the Quran presents in these verses of the universe and the world of humanity! Not only this world, but the Hereafter is also the "Day of Account" (Yawm al-Hisab) and the day of "setting the balances." In fact, the account and balance there are to degrees more precise than here. For this reason, we are commanded in Islamic narrations to take account of ourselves before we are called to account, and to place ourselves in the balance of measurement before we are weighed: "Take account of yourselves before you are taken to account, and weigh yourselves before you are weighed."

[^1]: The speed of the Earth's orbit around the sun (translational motion) is stated to be 35 kilometers per second! Its rotational speed around itself is about 1600 kilometers per hour (in equatorial regions). [^1]: Refer to Volume 13 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 46 onwards, and also Volume 11, page 172 onwards. [^2]: We have provided an explanation on this in Volume 20, page 315 onwards (under verse 47 of Surah Fussilat).

Ayahs

خَلَقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِن صَلْصَـٰلٍ كَٱلْفَخَّارِ (١٤)
He created man out of dry clay, like the potter’s, (14)
وَخَلَقَ ٱلْجَآنَّ مِن مَّارِجٍ مِّن نَّارٍ (١٥)
and created the jinn out of a flame of a fire. (15)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (١٦)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (16)
رَبُّ ٱلْمَشْرِقَيْنِ وَرَبُّ ٱلْمَغْرِبَيْنِ (١٧)
Lord of the two easts, and Lord of the two wests! (17)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (١٨)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (18)

Commentary

The Creation of Man from Clay Like Pottery!

After mentioning previous blessings, including the creation of man in a general sense, Allah begins in the verses under discussion by providing a detailed account of the creation of mankind and jinn—a description that serves both as a sign of His immense power and as a lesson for all to reflect upon.

He says: "He created man from dried clay like [that of] pottery" (خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ صَلْصالٍ كَالْفَخَّارِ).

"Salṣāl" (صلصال) originally means "the resonance of sound in dry bodies." Subsequently, it was applied to dried earth that makes a sound when tapped. The remains of water in a vessel are also called "ṣalṣah" because it makes a sound when moving from side to side.

Some have also said that "ṣalṣāl" means "foul-smelling mud" (ooze), but the first meaning is more famous and well-known.

"Fakhkhār" (فخار) is derived from the root "fakhr," meaning one who boasts much. Since such individuals are often hollow and noisy, this word was applied to jars and any kind of "pottery" (baked clay) because of the significant sound it produces! [^1] From various verses of the Quran and the different expressions used regarding the origin of human creation, it is clearly understood that man was initially dust (Surah Al-Hajj, Verse 5), then mixed with water to become mud (Al-An'am, 2), then became "foul-smelling mud" (Al-Hijr, 28), then acquired a "sticky" consistency (As-Saffat, 11), and finally became "dried," taking the form of "ṣalṣālin kal-fakhkhār" (the verse under discussion).

How long did these stages last in terms of time? How long did man remain in each stage? And under what factors did these transitional states occur? These are matters hidden from our knowledge, known only to Allah and none else.

[^1]: "Al-Mufradat" by Raghib.

What is certain is that these expressions convey a reality closely linked to human educational and spiritual development: that the primary material of man was very worthless and insignificant—among the lowliest materials on earth—yet the Great Allah created such a precious creature from such worthless material that he became the pinnacle of creation.

Furthermore, it is an allusion to the fact that the true value of man is constituted by the "Divine Spirit" and the Lordly breath mentioned in other verses of the Quran (such as Verse 29 of Surah Al-Hijr), so that by recognizing this truth, he may properly find his path to perfection and know which direction to take to regain his true value in the universe.

Then, He addresses the creation of the "jinn," saying: "And He created the jinn from a smokeless flame of fire" (وَ خَلَقَ الْجَانَّ مِنْ مارِجٍ مِنْ نارٍ).

"Mārij" (مارج) is originally from "marj" (on the weight of 'maradh'), meaning mixture and blending. Here, it refers to the mixing of different flames of fire, for when fire blazes, it sometimes appears red, sometimes yellow, sometimes blue, and sometimes white.

Some have said that the meaning of "movement" is also present in it (from "amrajat al-dābbah," meaning "I released the animal in the pasture," as one of the meanings of "marj" is pasture).

Again, it is not exactly clear to us how the creation of the "jinn" from these multicolored fires took place, just as its other characteristics have been established for us through the "True Dawn"—that is, the Holy Quran and Heavenly Revelation. The limitation of our knowledge compared to the unknown never allows us to deny or ignore these truths once they are proven through revelation, even if science finds no way to them.

(God willing, a more detailed explanation regarding the creation of jinn and the characteristics of this creature will come in the commentary of Surah Al-Jinn). In any case, the substances we deal with most are water, earth, air, and fire—whether we consider them simple elements like the ancients or composed of various parts like modern scientists. In any case, the origin of "man's" creation was water and earth, while the origin of the "jinn's" creation is air and fire, and this duality of origin is the source of many differences between the two.

Following the blessings present at the beginning of human creation, He repeats this sentence: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

In the next verse, He mentions another of the Divine blessings, saying: "He is the Lord of the two sunrises and the Lord of the two sunsets" (رَبُّ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ وَ رَبُّ الْمَغْرِبَيْنِ).

It is true that the sun rises from a certain point and sets at a certain point every day of the year, and thus has as many sunrises and sunsets as there are days in the year. However, considering the maximum "northern declination" of the sun and its "southern declination," it effectively has two sunrises and two sunsets, with the rest situated between these two. [^1] This system, which is the origin of the four seasons with their abundant blessings, is in fact a confirmation and completion of what appeared in previous verses—where the calculated movement of the sun and moon was mentioned, as well as the existence of a "balance" (mīzān) in the creation of the heavens. Collectively, it expresses the precise system of creation and the movement of the earth, moon, and sun, while also pointing to the blessings and benefits that accrue to man through this.

Some have interpreted the "two sunrises and two sunsets" here as the rising and setting of the "sun" and the rising and setting of the "moon," finding it consistent with the previous verse "the sun and the moon [move] by precise calculation." However, the first meaning seems more appropriate, especially since it has been mentioned in some Islamic narrations.

Among them, we read in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, that in interpreting this verse he said:

"The sunrise of winter is distinct, and the sunrise of summer is distinct; do you not recognize that from the closeness and distance of the sun?" (Referring to the sun rising higher in the sky in summer and lower in winter). [^2]

[^1]: Explanation: Since the Earth's axis is tilted relative to the plane of its orbit, forming an angle of about 23 degrees, and it orbits the sun in this state, the sunrise and sunset appear constantly changing. It varies from 23 degrees maximum northern declination (at the start of summer) to 23 degrees maximum southern declination (at the start of winter). The first orbit is called the "Tropic of Cancer" and the second the "Tropic of Capricorn," and these are the two sunrises and two sunsets of the sun, with all other orbits located within these two.

[^2]: Tafsir "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 190.

From what we have said, it becomes clear why it is mentioned in some verses of the Quran: "So I swear by the Lord of the sunrises and sunsets" (فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِرَبِّ الْمَشارِقِ وَ الْمَغارِبِ) (Al-Ma'arij, 40).

For there, it refers to all the sunrises and sunsets of the sun throughout the year, whereas in the verse under discussion, it refers only to the extremities of its ascending and descending arcs.

In any case, after mentioning this blessing, He again addresses the jinn and mankind, saying: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

Ayahs

مَرَجَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ يَلْتَقِيَانِ (١٩)
He merged the two seas, meeting each other. (19)
بَيْنَهُمَا بَرْزَخٌ لَّا يَبْغِيَانِ (٢٠)
There is a barrier between them, which they do not overstep. (20)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٢١)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (21)
يَخْرُجُ مِنْهُمَا ٱللُّؤْلُؤُ وَٱلْمَرْجَانُ (٢٢)
From them emerge the pearl and the coral. (22)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٢٣)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (23)
وَلَهُ ٱلْجَوَارِ ٱلْمُنشَـَٔاتُ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ كَٱلْأَعْلَـٰمِ (٢٤)
His are the sailing ships on the sea [appearing] like landmarks. (24)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٢٥)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (25)

Commentary

Seas with Their Precious Treasures

Continuing the description of the Lord's blessings, the discourse turns to the seas—not all seas, but a specific phenomenon in certain parts of them. This is both a wondrous occurrence and a sign of the infinite power of the Truth, as well as a means for the production of certain goods used by human beings.

He says: "He released the two seas, meeting [side by side]" (مَرَجَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ يَلْتَقِيانِ).

"But between them is a barrier [so] neither of them transgresses [or prevails over the other]" (بَيْنَهُما بَرْزَخٌ لا يَبْغِيانِ).

The root maraja (on the weight of falaj) means to mix, or to send and release. Here, it means to send and place side by side, as evidenced by the phrase "between them is a barrier [so] neither of them transgresses."

Based on the testimony of verse 53 of Surah al-Furqan, these two seas refer to "fresh" and "salt" water, where He says: وَ هُوَ الَّذِي مَرَجَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ هذا عَذْبٌ فُراتٌ وَ هذا مِلْحٌ أُجاجٌ وَ جَعَلَ بَيْنَهُما بَرْزَخاً وَ حِجْراً مَحْجُوراً:

"And it is He who has released the two seas, one palatable and sweet and the other salty and bitter, and He placed between them a barrier and a prohibited partition."

However, regarding where these two seas of fresh and salt water are located such that they do not prevail over one another, and what this "barrier" between them is, there is much discussion among commentators. Some have offered interpretations suggesting that the state of the earth's seas was not fully clear to them at that time. For instance, some said these two seas refer to the Persian Sea and the Roman Sea, whereas today we know both these seas contain salt water and there is no barrier between them.

Others said it refers to the sea of the sky and the sea of the earth—the first being fresh and the second salty—while we know there is no sea in the sky except for clouds and vapors rising from the earth's oceans.

Still others suggested it refers to underground fresh waters that do not mix with seawater, and the barrier between them is the walls of these reservoirs.

However, we know that very little exists underground in the form of a "sea"; rather, water particles are hidden and concealed between particles of soil and moist sand. When a well is dug at a certain point, this moisture gradually collects and water appears. Furthermore, pearls and coral, mentioned in the subsequent verses, are not obtained from underground waters.

So, what is meant by these two seas?

Previously, in the commentary of Surah al-Furqan, we pointed out the fact that when great rivers of fresh water flow into seas and oceans, they usually form a sea of fresh water along the coast, pushing the salt water back. Remarkably, for a long time, these two waters—fresh and salt—do not mix due to the difference in their density.

When traveling by plane over areas where these rivers flow into the sea, the sight of the fresh and salt water seas lying side by side yet remaining distinct is clearly visible from above. As the edges of these waters eventually mix, new fresh water takes their place, so that these two distinct seas constantly attract attention.

Interestingly, during high tide when the ocean level rises, the fresh waters are pushed back without mixing with the salt water (except during times of drought and low water levels) and cover a large portion of the land. Consequently, coastal dwellers in these regions harness these fresh waters to create many streams in the coastal area, through which vast lands are irrigated.

These streams, created by the blessing of the coastal tides and their effect on the water of these rivers, fill and empty with fresh water twice every twenty-four hours, serving as a very effective means for irrigating vast regions.

Regarding these two seas, another interesting interpretation has been offered by some, suggesting it likely refers to the "Gulf Stream," the details of which we will explain, Allah willing, in the "Key Points" following these verses.

Once again, He addresses His servants and asks them in the face of these blessings: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

Then, continuing the same discourse, He adds: "From both of them emerge pearl and coral" (يَخْرُجُ مِنْهُمَا اللُّؤْلُؤُ وَ الْمَرْجانُ).

"So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

"Pearl" and "coral" are two attractive means of adornment and are also used in medicine and the treatment of diseases. Additionally, they are excellent trade commodities from which significant revenue is obtained. For these reasons, they are mentioned as two blessings in the above verses.

"Pearl" (lu'lu'), known in Persian as morvarid, is a transparent and precious bead that develops inside a shell in the depths of the seas; the larger it is, the more valuable it becomes. It has extensive applications in medicine. Ancient physicians prepared medicines from it to strengthen the heart and nerves, to eliminate various types of palpitations and states of fear and terror, to strengthen the liver, and to remove bad breath, kidney and bladder stones, and jaundice. It was even used for eye diseases [1].

"Coral" (marjan) has been interpreted by some as small pearls, but the reality is different. Coral is a living organism resembling a small tree branch that grows in the depths of the seas. For a long time, scientists thought it was a type of "plant," but it was later revealed to be a type of animal, even though it attaches to rocks on the seabed. Sometimes it covers a vast area and gradually increases, forming islands known as "coral islands."

Coral usually grows in still waters, and divers harvest it from the coasts of the Red Sea, the Mediterranean, and other locations.

The best type of decorative coral is "red coral," and the redder it is, the more precious it becomes; the metaphors used by poets in their verses are based on this.

[1]: Tohfeh-ye Hakim Mo'men, Dehkhoda Encyclopedia, and other sources.

The worst type of coral is "white coral," which is found in abundance, and between these two lies "black coral."

In addition to its decorative aspect, coral has medicinal uses, and many properties have been attributed to it. These include making medicines to strengthen the heart, counteract viper venom, strengthen nerves, and treat diarrhea and uterine hemorrhages. It is also said to be beneficial for epilepsy [1].

It is also necessary to mention that some commentators have stated that pearls and coral only grow in salt water; therefore, they faced difficulty in interpreting the verse "From both of them emerge pearl and coral" (يَخْرُجُ مِنْهُمَا اللُّؤْلُؤُ وَ الْمَرْجانُ) and suggested it refers to only one of the two (similar to verse 31 of Az-Zukhruf).

However, we have no evidence for this; rather, some have explicitly stated that pearls and coral are found in both fresh and salt waters.

Continuing this section on blessings, He refers to the matter of ships, which have truly been the greatest and most important means of human transportation in the past and present, saying: "And His are the ships, [with sails] raised, in the sea like mountains" (وَ لَهُ الْجَوارِ الْمُنْشَآتُ فِي الْبَحْرِ كَالْأَعْلامِ).

Jawar is the plural of jariyah, a description for sufun meaning "ships," which has been omitted from the phrase for brevity, because what is most noteworthy is the flowing and movement of the ships; hence, this specific attribute is emphasized (take note).

[1]: Farid Wajdi Encyclopedia and other books.

A "handmaiden" is called jariyah because of her movement and effort in performing services, and a young girl is called jariyah because of the flow of youthful vitality in her being.

Munsha'at is the plural of munsha', the passive participle of insha' meaning "to create." Interestingly, while using the term munsha'at which indicates the ship being manufactured by man, He says "and His" (وَ لَهُ), referring to the fact that inventors and shipbuilders utilize God-given properties in the various materials used in ships. They also benefit from the fluid nature of seawater and the force of the winds, and it is God who created these properties and effects in those materials, in the sea, and in the wind.

Therefore, elsewhere in the Quran, the term "subjected" (taskhir) is used: وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْفُلْكَ لِتَجْرِيَ فِي الْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِ: "And He subjected for you the ships to sail through the sea by His command" (Ibrahim 32).

Some have interpreted munsha' from the root insha' meaning "to raise high," considering it a reference to sailing ships that used the raising of sails and placing them in the path of winds as the motive force for the ships.

A'lam is the plural of 'alam (on the weight of qalam) meaning "mountain," although originally it means a sign or mark that conveys information about something. Since mountains are visible from afar, they are referred to as 'alam, just as a flag is also called 'alam.

In this way, the Quran emphasizes large ships that move upon the surface of oceans and seas. Contrary to what some imagine, large ships are not exclusive to the age of motors and steam; in the history of the wars of the Greeks and other nations, we read that they used very large troop-carrying ships.

And once again, He repeats this meaningful question, saying: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

Key Points

The Sea: A Hub of Divine Blessings

As we have seen, this section of verses refers to the sea and its importance in human life. We know that seas cover approximately three-quarters of the Earth's surface and are a vast source of food, medicine, and ornaments, as well as a vital route for the transportation of people and goods. Most importantly, the falling of rain, the moderation of climate, and even part of the wind currents are among the blessings of the seas.

If the surface area of the seas were less or more than it is now, the Earth would either become a desert or become so humid that it would be uninhabitable! Therefore, the Quran repeatedly and in various expressions draws human attention to this great blessing and prompts them to reflect. Sometimes it says: "Allah subjected the sea for you" (Jathiyah 12).

Sometimes it says: "He subjected the ships for you" (Ibrahim 32).

And sometimes it says: "He subjected for you whatever is on the earth" (Hajj 65).

Beyond all this, the sea is a realm of wonders. The smallest microscopic plants and the tallest trees in the world grow in the seas. Likewise, the smallest animals and the most massive giant creatures live in the seas! Life in the depths of the seas, where there is neither light nor food, is so amazing that one never tires of studying it. Remarkably, animals there emit their own light, and their food is produced at the water's surface and settles down. Their bodies are built so strong, resilient, and with internal pressure that they withstand that immense water pressure—which, if a human were placed there normally, their bones would be turned to powder!

2- The Gulf Stream and Great Oceanic Rivers!

If you are not surprised, throughout the world's oceans, great rivers are in motion, one of the most powerful of which is called the "Gulf Stream."

This massive river moves from the coasts of Central America, traverses the entire Atlantic Ocean, and reaches the coasts of Northern Europe.

These waters, moving from regions near the equator, are warm, and even their color is sometimes different from the adjacent waters. Amazingly, the width of this great oceanic river (the Gulf Stream) is about 150 kilometers, and its depth is several hundred meters! Its speed in some areas is such that it travels 160 kilometers in a single day.

The temperature difference between these waters and the adjacent waters is about 10 to 15 degrees; hence, its western edge is called the "cold wall."

The Gulf Stream creates warm winds and carries a significant amount of its heat toward the northern countries of the European continent, making the climate of those countries very pleasant. Perhaps if this current did not exist, life in those countries would be very difficult and unbearable.

We repeat: the Gulf Stream is just one of these rivers, and in the waters of the five continents, there are many such oceanic currents.

The primary cause is the temperature difference between the Earth's equatorial region and the polar regions, which creates this movement in the seawater.

This subject can be understood with a simple experiment: if we have a large container of water, place a piece of ice on one side and a piece of hot iron on the other, and sprinkle some straw on the water's surface, we will see a current appear on the surface, and the water slowly moves from the warm region toward the cold region. This exact phenomenon occurs in all the world's seas and is the source of these oceanic rivers.

Amazingly, these great oceanic rivers mix very little with their surrounding waters and travel thousands of kilometers in the same state, creating an instance of "He released the two seas, meeting [side by side]; between them is a barrier [so] neither of them transgresses"! Even more interesting is that at the meeting point of these warm waters with the adjacent cold waters, a phenomenon occurs that is very beneficial to humans. At the intersection of these warm and cold waters, a kind of numbness or mass death occurs for the microscopic animals suspended in the water, and through this, an abundant and countless supply of food accumulates, attracting large schools of fish. Thus, this region becomes one of the best fishing grounds on Earth [1].

[1]: Farhang-nameh Encyclopedia, Vol. 12, p. 1228; Bandar va Darya Magazine, No. 4, p. 100; and other sources.

Therefore, one of the interpretations for the above verses is this, and it does not contradict other interpretations; a combination of all of them is possible.

3- An Interpretation from the Inner Meanings of the Verses

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, regarding the interpretation of the verse "He released the two seas, meeting [side by side]..." he said:

"Ali and Fatimah (عليهما السلام) are two deep seas, neither of which transgresses against the other; 'From both of them emerge pearl and coral,' he said: [meaning] al-Hasan and al-Husayn (عليهما السلام)." [1]

In the commentary al-Durr al-Manthur, this same meaning is narrated from some of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله [2]. The late Tabarsi also narrated it in Majma' al-Bayan with a slight variation. We know that the Holy Quran has inner meanings (butun), and a single verse may have several meanings, or even dozens of meanings. What is mentioned in this Hadith is from the inner meanings of the Quran, which does not contradict its apparent meaning.

[1]: Tafsir al-Qummi, Vol. 2, p. 344. [2]: al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 142.

Ayahs

كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْهَا فَانٍ (٢٦)
Everyone on it is ephemeral, (26)
وَيَبْقَىٰ وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ذُو ٱلْجَلَـٰلِ وَٱلْإِكْرَامِ (٢٧)
yet lasting is the majestic and munificent Face of your Lord. (27)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٢٨)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (28)
يَسْـَٔلُهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِى شَأْنٍ (٢٩)
Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him. Every day He is engaged in some work. (29)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٣٠)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (30)

Commentary

All are Perishing, and Subsistence is Yours Alone!

Continuing the description of Divine blessings, these verses add: "Everyone upon the earth will perish" (كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْها فانٍ).

But how can the "issue of perishing" be counted among Divine blessings?

It may be from the perspective that this perishing is not absolute annihilation; rather, it is a gateway to the realm of subsistence, a corridor and a passage through which crossing is a condition for reaching the eternal abode.

The world, with all its blessings, is a prison for the believer, and exiting this world is a liberation from this narrow and dark prison.

Or, it may be from the perspective that the mention of the previous abundant blessings might cause some to become heedless and drowned in worldly life—its various foods, drinks, pearls, corals, and swift mounts. Therefore, He reminds them that this world is not a place of permanence; lest you develop an attachment to these things and fail to utilize them in the path of the Lord. This reminder itself is a great blessing.

The pronoun in "alayha" (upon it) refers to the earth, which was alluded to in the previous verses; furthermore, it is clear from the context.

The meaning of "those who are upon the earth" (man alayha) refers to humans and jinn, although some commentators have suggested it includes animals, creeping things, and other living creatures. However, the literal use of "man" (who), which is usually reserved for rational beings, supports the first meaning.

It is true that the issue of "perishing" is not limited to humans and jinn, nor even to earthly creatures; rather, according to the explicit text of the Quran, all inhabitants of the heavens and all beings shall perish: "Everything is perishing except His Face" (Al-Qasas, 88). However, since the discourse was about the inhabitants of the earth, this verse mentions only them.

In the next verse, it adds: "And there remains the Face of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor" (وَ يَبْقى‌ وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ذُو الْجَلالِ وَ الْإِكْرامِ).

"Wajh" (Face) literally means the countenance with which one confronts another, but when used regarding Allah, it refers to His Pure Essence.

Some have also interpreted "the Face of your Lord" here to mean the Attributes of the Lord through which blessings and favors descend upon humanity, such as Knowledge, Power, Mercy, and Forgiveness.

Another possibility is that it refers to deeds performed for the sake of God; thus, everything perishes except those deeds performed with sincerity of intention and for His pleasure.

However, the first meaning seems most appropriate of all.

As for "Owner of Majesty and Honor" (ذُو الْجَلالِ وَ الْإِكْرامِ), which is a description for "Face," it refers to the Attributes of Beauty and Majesty of God. "Dhu al-Jalal" (Owner of Majesty) informs of attributes from which Allah is "Ajal" (Exalted) and above (the Negative Attributes), while "Ikram" (Honor) refers to attributes that manifest the beauty and value of something, which are the Positive Attributes of God, such as His Knowledge, Power, and Life.

Therefore, the overall meaning of the verse is: only the Pure Essence of God, who is characterized by Positive Attributes and sanctified from Negative Attributes, remains and persists in this world.

Some commentators also consider God being the "Owner of Honor" as an allusion to the favors and blessings with which He honors and dignifies His friends (Awliya). A combination of these meanings in the above verse is also possible.

In a Hadith, we read that a man was praying in the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and then supplicated: "O Allah, I ask You, for to You belongs all praise; there is no deity but You, the Bestower, Originator of the heavens and the earth, Owner of Majesty and Honor, O Ever-Living, O Sustainer."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to his companions: "Do you know by what name he called upon God?" They replied: "Allah and His Messenger know best." He said:

"By the One in whose hand is my soul, he has called upon Allah by His Greatest Name (Ism al-A'zam), which if He is called by it, He responds, and if He is asked by it, He grants." [1]

Once again, addressing the creation, He says: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

The content of the next verse is, in fact, a result of the previous verses, for it says: "All those who are in the heavens and the earth constantly ask Him for their needs" (يَسْئَلُهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ).

Why should it not be so? While all are perishing and He is Subsisting. Not only at the end of the world will all beings tread the path of annihilation except the Pure Essence of the Lord, but even now, all are "perishing" before Him; their subsistence is tied to His subsistence and His Will. If He were to withdraw His gaze of grace from the creation for a single moment, "the forms would collapse"! In such a state, is there anyone other than Him whom the inhabitants of the heavens and earth should petition?

[1]: Tafsir "Ruh al-Ma'ani," Vol. 27, p. 95.

The use of "yas'aluhu" (asks Him) in the "present/future tense" (mudari') is evidence that this asking and petitioning is continuous. All, by the "tongue of their state," constantly seek grace from that Overflowing Source; they desire existence and plead for their needs. This is the requirement of the essence of the "contingent being"—that not only in "origination" (huduth) but also in "subsistence" (baqa), they are dependent upon and reliant on the "Necessary Being" (Wajib al-Wujud).

Then it adds: "Every day He is in a [new] state/affair" (كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِي شَأْنٍ).

Yes, His creation is continuous and ongoing, and His responding to the needs of the petitioners and the needy is likewise. Every day He originates a new plan.

One day He gives power to nations, another day He brings them down to the black dust. One day He grants health and youth, another day He gives weakness and frailty. One day He removes grief and sorrow from a heart, another day He creates a cause for sorrow. In short, every day, according to Wisdom and the Best Order, He has a new phenomenon, a new creation, and a new event.

Attention to this reality, on one hand, clarifies our continuous need for His Pure Essence, and on the other hand, pushes aside the veils of despair and hopelessness from the heart.

And on a third hand, it shatters pride and heedlessness.

Yes, every day He is in a [new] state.

Although commentators have each mentioned a corner of this vast meaning as an interpretation of the verse:

Some mentioned only the forgiveness of sins, the removal of sorrows, and the rise and fall of nations.

Some mentioned only the issue of creation, sustenance, life, death, honor, and humiliation.

Others mentioned only the issue of the creation and death of humans, saying:

God has three armies every day: an army transferred from the loins of fathers to the wombs of mothers, an army stepping from the wombs of mothers into the world, and an army departing from this world toward the grave.

But as we said, the verse has a broad concept that encompasses every kind of fresh creation, new origination, change, and transformation in this world.

In a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read that in one of his sermons he said:

"Praise be to Allah who does not die, and whose wonders do not end, because 'Every day He is in a state' of originating wonders that never were." [1]

In another Hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that in interpreting this verse he said:

"Of His affairs is that He forgives a sin, relieves a distress, raises a people, and lowers others." [2]

It is also necessary to remind that "yawm" (day) here does not mean a day as opposed to night; rather, it includes long eras as well as hours and moments. Its meaning is that Allah Almighty is in a state and affair at every moment.

Some have also mentioned a "reason for revelation" (shan al-nuzul) for the verse, stating that it is a rebuttal to the claims of

[1]: "Usul al-Kafi" as quoted in Tafsir "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 193. [2]: "Majma' al-Bayan" under the verses in question—this Hadith is also quoted in "Ruh al-Ma'ani" from "Sahih Bukhari."

the Jews, who believed that God ceases all work on "Saturday" (the Sabbath) and issues no decree or command! [1] The Quran says: the program of His creation and governance does not admit a moment of cessation.

And again, following this continuous blessing and the responding to the needs of all creatures and the inhabitants of the heavens and earth, it repeats: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

Key Points

1- What is the Reality of "Perishing" (Fana)?

What we read in the above verses—that everyone except God perishes—does not mean absolute non-existence in the sense that even the human soul is annihilated, or the dust resulting from his body becomes non-existent. This is because Quranic verses explicitly state the existence of the "Barzakh" (isthmus) until the Day of Resurrection [2], and on the other hand, it repeatedly says that the dead will rise from their graves on the Day of Judgment [3], and decayed bones (idham ramim) will don the raiment of life by God's command [4]. All these are evidence that "perishing" in this verse and similar verses means the dismantling of the system of body and soul, the severing of connections, the disruption of the order of the world of creation,

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 202. [2]: Al-Mu'minun, 100. [3]: Ya-Sin, 51. [4]: Ya-Sin, 79.

and the replacement of that world with another.

2- Every Day He Creates a New Phenomenon

We said the verse "Every day He is in a state" is both hope-inspiring and pride-shattering, as well as a sign of the permanence of creation and the continuity of origination. For this reason, the leaders of Islam sometimes relied specifically on this verse to give hope to individuals. As we read in the story of the painful exile of Abu Dharr to "Rabadhah": Ali عليه السلام comforted him with very captivating and meaningful sentences while seeing him off. Then Imam Hasan عليه السلام, the brave son of the Commander of the Faithful, addressed him as "Uncle" and added other sentences. After him, the Master of Martyrs, Imam Husayn عليه السلام, spoke and said:

"O Uncle! Indeed, Allah Almighty is capable of changing what you see; 'Every day He is in a state.' These people denied you their world, and you denied them your religion... so ask Allah for patience and victory." [1]

We also read that when Imam Husayn عليه السلام was coming toward Karbala, when he reached the station of "Sifah," the poet Farazdaq met the Imam عليه السلام. The Imam عليه السلام said: "Explain to me the news of the people behind you" (referring to the people of Iraq). He replied: "You have asked one who is informed; the hearts of the people are with you, but their swords are with the Umayyads! And the Decree descends from heaven, and Allah does what He wills."

Imam Husayn عليه السلام said: "You have spoken the truth; the matter belongs to Allah, He does what He wills, and every day our Lord is in a state." [2] All these show that this verse is a hope-inspiring verse for the believers.

Interestingly, it is said that one of the emirs asked his vizier for the interpretation of this verse, but he expressed ignorance and asked for a deadline until the next day.

When he returned home sad and grieved, he had a (wise) black servant who asked: "What is the news?" He told the story. The servant said: "Go to the emir; if he wishes, I will explain the interpretation of the verse to him." The emir summoned him and asked him. In response, he said:

"O Emir! His state is that He merges the night into the day, and merges the day into the night; He brings the living out of the dead, and brings the dead out of the living; He heals the sick and makes the healthy sick; He afflicts the sound and grants well-being to the afflicted; He honors the humiliated and humiliates the honored; He impoverishes the rich and enriches the poor!" The emir said: "You have relieved me, may Allah relieve you," and then he honored him and gave him a reward. [1]

3- Substantial Motion (Al-Harakat al-Jawhariyyah)

Some proponents of "Substantial Motion" have argued from Quranic verses, or at least consider them an allusion to their objective, including this very verse: "Every day He is in a state."

To explain: The ancient philosophers believed that motion is only possible in four accidental categories (the categories of "where," "quantity," "quality," and "position"). In simpler terms, it is possible for a body to change its location, or to grow and increase in quantity, or for example, its color, smell, and taste (like an apple on a tree) to change, or to rotate in its place (like the earth's diurnal motion). But they believed that motion is never possible in the "substance" (jawhar) and essence of the body, because in every motion, the essence of the moving thing must remain constant while its accidents change; otherwise, motion would have no meaning.

However, later philosophers rejected this theory and believed in "Substantial Motion." They said: the basis of motion is in the essence and substance, the effects of which appear in the accidents.

The first person to present this view in a detailed and reasoned manner was Mulla Sadra of Shiraz. He said: all particles of the universe and the world of matter are entirely motion; or in other words, the matter of bodies is a "fluid existence" whose essence is constantly changing. Every moment is a new existence that differs from the previous existence. But because these

[1]: "Al-Ghadir," Vol. 8, p. 301. [2]: "Kamil ibn Athir," Vol. 4, p. 40. [1]: "Tafsir al-Qurtubi," Vol. 9, p. 6337.

changes are connected, it is considered a single thing. Therefore, we are a new existence every moment, but these existences are connected and continuous, having a single form. Or in other words: matter has four dimensions: length, width, depth, and another dimension we call "time," and this time is nothing but the measure of motion in the substance (pay close attention).

Let it not be misunderstood: Substantial Motion has no connection to the issue of motion within atoms, for that is motion in "place" and motion in "accident."

Substantial Motion has a much deeper concept that includes the essence and identity of the body. Amazingly, here the "mover" becomes identical to the "motion," and things become corridors for themselves! (pay close attention).

They have numerous arguments to prove this objective, which is not the place to explain here. However, it is not bad to mention the result of this philosophical belief: the result is that we understand the issue of "Knowing God" more clearly than ever. For creation and origination were not only at the beginning of the world; rather, every hour and every moment is a beginning, and God is constantly engaged in fresh creation and new origination. We are constantly dependent on Him and receiving grace from the grace of His Essence. They have also interpreted "Every day He is in a state" in this sense. Of course, there is no obstacle to this being a part of the broad concept of the verse.

Ayahs

سَنَفْرُغُ لَكُمْ أَيُّهَ ٱلثَّقَلَانِ (٣١)
Soon We shall make Ourselves unoccupied for you, O you notable two! (31)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٣٢)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (32)
يَـٰمَعْشَرَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ إِنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتُمْ أَن تَنفُذُوا۟ مِنْ أَقْطَارِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنفُذُوا۟ ۚ لَا تَنفُذُونَ إِلَّا بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ (٣٣)
O company of jinn and humans! If you can pass through the confines of the heavens and the earth, then do pass through. But you will not pass through except by an authority [from Allah]. (33)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٣٤)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (34)
يُرْسَلُ عَلَيْكُمَا شُوَاظٌ مِّن نَّارٍ وَنُحَاسٌ فَلَا تَنتَصِرَانِ (٣٥)
There will be unleashed upon you a flash of fire and a smoke; then you will not be able to help one another. (35)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٣٦)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (36)

Commentary

If You Can, Pass Through the Confines of the Heavens!

The blessings mentioned so far in the verses of this Surah have pertained to this world. However, the verses under discussion speak of the reckoning of the Resurrection and other characteristics of the Hereafter. While these serve as a threat to the criminals, they are also a means of education, awareness, and awakening, as well as a source of encouragement and heartening for the believers. For this reason, they are considered blessings; hence, after mentioning each one, the same question regarding blessings is repeated.

First, He says: "We shall soon attend to your reckoning, O you two heavy groups [of jinn and men]!" (سَنَفْرُغُ لَكُمْ أَيُّهَ الثَّقَلانِ) [^1] [^2]. Indeed, on that day, the All-Knowing and All-Powerful God will precisely examine and account for all the deeds, words, and intentions of mankind and jinn, determining the appropriate punishment or reward for them.

Although Allah, whenever He attends to something, is never heedless of another, and possesses simultaneous scientific encompassment over all creation—and nothing ever distracts Him from something else ("لا يشغله شان عن شان")—the expression "سَنَفْرُغُ" (We shall settle/empty Ourselves for you) is striking. This expression is typically used when a person sets aside all their tasks to attend to a specific matter with full focus. This applies only to creatures who, due to their limitations, are diverted from one thing when they turn their attention to another.

However, in the case of Allah, this expression is nothing but an ultimate emphasis on the matter of auditing the accounts, such that not even an atom's weight will be overlooked. How wondrous it is that the Great God takes upon Himself the accounting of His small servants, and how terrifying and awe-inspiring is such a reckoning!
