"Thaqalan" is derived from the root "thiql" (with the weight of kibr), meaning a heavy load, and it also means weight. However, "thaqal" (with the weight of khabar) usually refers to the baggage and belongings of a traveler. Its application to the groups of jinn and men is due to their spiritual "weight," as Allah has given them a specific weight and value in terms of intellect, consciousness, science, and awareness—even though, physically, they also possess a significant collective weight. Thus, we read in Surah Az-Zalzalah, verse 2: "وَ أَخْرَجَتِ الْأَرْضُ أَثْقالَها" (And the earth throws out its burdens), one meaning of which is the emergence of humans from their graves at the time of Resurrection. In any case, the expression in the verse under discussion focuses more on the spiritual aspect, especially since the group of jinn does not seem to have much physical weight.

It is also worth noting that the specific mention of these two groups is because they are the primary ones bound by divine obligations.

After mentioning this meaning, He repeats the question again: "Then which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

Following the previous verse which spoke of the precise divine reckoning, He addresses the jinn and men again, saying: "O assembly of jinn and men! If you truly wish to remain beyond the reach of divine punishment and retribution, then if you can, pass beyond the zones of the heavens and the earth and exit the sphere of His power; but you will never be able to do so except with a [divine] authority, and such divine power is not at your disposal" (يا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ وَ الْإِنْسِ إِنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ أَنْ تَنْفُذُوا مِنْ أَقْطارِ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ فَانْفُذُوا لا تَنْفُذُونَ إِلَّا بِسُلْطانٍ).

In this way, you have no power to flee from the court of God's justice and its consequences. Wherever you go is God's kingdom, and wherever you are is the domain of His rule. Indeed, where can this weak and helpless creature flee from the arena of Allah's power? As the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, supplicates in the soul-nourishing Dua Kumayl:

"و لا يمكن الفرار من حكومتك" (And escape from Your government is impossible.)

"Ma'shar" is originally derived from "ashr," meaning the number ten. Since ten is a complete number, the word "ma'shar" is used for a complete assembly composed of various classes and groups.

"Aqtar" is the plural of "qutr," meaning the sides or boundaries of something.

"Tanfudhu" is from the root nufudh, which originally means piercing or passing through something. The expression "min aqtar" (from the zones/diameters) indicates piercing the sides of the heavens, passing through them, and traveling beyond them.

Additionally, placing "jinn" before "men" here may be because they have a greater readiness for traversing the heavens.

There is a discussion among commentators as to whether the above verse pertains to the Resurrection, this world, or both. Since the verses before and after it focus on the events of the next world, it seems that this verse also relates to fleeing from the clutches of divine justice on the Day of Judgment.

However, some, noting the phrase "لا تَنْفُذُونَ إِلَّا بِسُلْطانٍ" (you cannot pass except with authority/power), have considered it an allusion to human space travel, for which the Quran stipulates the possession of scientific and industrial authority.

The possibility has also been raised that it refers to both the Resurrection and this world—meaning you are neither able to penetrate the zones of the heavens here without power, nor there. However, here a limited means is placed at your disposal, while there, no means exists.

Some have also offered a fourth interpretation: that it refers to intellectual and scientific penetration into the zones of the heavens, which is possible for man through the power of reasoning.

But the first interpretation seems most appropriate, supported by some reports narrated in Islamic sources. Among them is a hadith from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام: "On the Day of Resurrection, Allah will gather the servants in one place and reveal to the angels of the lowest heaven to descend. They, who are twice the population of jinn and men on earth, will descend. Then the inhabitants of the second heaven, who are also twice the total, will descend, and likewise the angels of the seven heavens will descend, surrounding the jinn and men like seven veils. It is then that a caller will cry out: 'O assembly of jinn and men, if you can pass through the zones of the heavens and the earth, then do so, but you never can except by divine power.' And here they will see that they are surrounded by seven massive groups of angels (and there is no way to escape the clutches of justice)." [^3] A combination of these interpretations is also possible.

Again, He addresses the two groups here, saying: "Then which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ). It is true that the above threat is outwardly in the path of punishment and retribution, but since its mention is a warning to all humans and a factor for reform and education, it is naturally considered a grace and a blessing. Fundamentally, the existence of accounting in any system is a great blessing, for because of it, everyone's affairs will be held to account!

The next verse, to emphasize what was stated in the previous verse regarding the inability of jinn and men to flee the clutches of justice, adds: "There will be sent against you both flames of smokeless fire and thick smoke (and they will surround you from every side such that there is no way to escape), and then you will not be able to seek help from anyone" (يُرْسَلُ عَلَيْكُما شُواظٌ مِنْ نارٍ وَ نُحاسٌ فَلا تَنْتَصِرانِ). On one hand, the angels surround you, and on the other, hot, burning flames of fire and dark, suffocating smoke encompass the area of the Gathering, leaving no way for escape.

"Shuwadh"—according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, Ibn Manzur in Lisan al-Arab, and many commentators—means smokeless flames of fire. Some have interpreted it as tongues of fire that appear detached from the fire itself and are green in color. In any case, this expression points to the intensity of its heat.

[^1]: It should be noted that in the old script of the Holy Quran, in a few instances, "ayyuha" is written as "ayyu-h" (ايه), which includes the verse under discussion and two other verses (An-Nur: 31 and Az-Zukhruf: 49), while in other cases, the script "ayyuha" is recorded with a long alif at the end. It seems this was based on a rule in the ancient script.

[^2]: Although "Al-Thaqalan" is dual, the pronoun in "lakum" appears in the plural form because it refers to two groups.

[^3]: Tafsir al-Safi, page 517; Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, vol. 9, page 205.

And "nuhas" means smoke (or red-colored, smoky flames of fire) that takes on the color of copper. Some have also interpreted it as "molten copper," which apparently has no relevance to the verse under discussion, as the talk is of something that surrounds man on the Day of Resurrection and prevents him from fleeing the court of divine justice.

How wondrous is the court of Resurrection, where man is completely surrounded from every side by divine agents, burning fire, and lethal smoke, having no choice but to attend that court and submit to the verdict of that tribunal.

He says again: "Then which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ). The use of the term "blessing" (favor) here is also due to the same grace mentioned in the previous verse.

Ayahs

فَإِذَا ٱنشَقَّتِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ فَكَانَتْ وَرْدَةً كَٱلدِّهَانِ (٣٧)
When the sky is split open, and turns crimson like tanned leather. (37)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٣٨)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (38)
فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لَّا يُسْـَٔلُ عَن ذَنۢبِهِۦٓ إِنسٌ وَلَا جَآنٌّ (٣٩)
On that day neither humans will be questioned about their sins nor jinn. (39)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٠)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (40)
يُعْرَفُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ فَيُؤْخَذُ بِٱلنَّوَٰصِى وَٱلْأَقْدَامِ (٤١)
The guilty will be recognized by their mark; so they will be seized by their forelocks and feet. (41)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٢)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (42)
هَـٰذِهِۦ جَهَنَّمُ ٱلَّتِى يُكَذِّبُ بِهَا ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٤٣)
‘This is the hell which the guilty would deny!’ (43)
يَطُوفُونَ بَيْنَهَا وَبَيْنَ حَمِيمٍ ءَانٍ (٤٤)
They shall circuit between it and boiling hot water. (44)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٥)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (45)

Commentary

Sinners are Recognized by Their Marks!

Following the previous verses that recounted some of the events of the Resurrection, these verses continue that same discussion, mentioning other characteristics of the scene of Judgment, the manner of accounting, and the punishment and retribution.

First, It says: "When the heaven is rent asunder and becomes rose-red like molten oil, terrifying events occur that no one has the power to endure" (فَإِذَا انْشَقَّتِ السَّماءُ فَكانَتْ وَرْدَةً كَالدِّهانِ) [^1]. From the totality of the verses regarding "Resurrection," it is clearly understood that on that day, the current order of the world will be completely disrupted, and horrific events will occur throughout the entire universe. The stars, planets, earth, and heaven will be transformed, and matters that are difficult for us to imagine today will take place. Among these is what is mentioned in the above verse: that the celestial spheres will split apart and turn a crimson color, becoming molten like oil.

"Wardah" (وَرْدَة) and "Ward" (وَرْد) mean "rose" or "flower," and since flowers are often red, it evokes the meaning of "red" here.

This word is also used to refer to reddish-colored horses; since such horses change color throughout the seasons—becoming slightly yellow in spring, red in winter, and dark in extreme cold—the transformations that the scene of the sky undergoes on the Day of Resurrection are likened to them: sometimes like a red and burning flame of fire, sometimes yellow, and sometimes black, smoky, and dark.

"Dihan" (دِهان) (on the pattern of Kitab) means molten oil, and sometimes refers to the dregs that settle in oil, which often have various colors. This comparison may be because the color of the sky becomes rose-red like molten oil, or it may refer to the melting of the celestial spheres, or their various colors. Some have also interpreted "Dihan" to mean "red leather." In any case, these metaphors can only visualize a shadow of that terrifying scene, for in reality, it bears no resemblance to any event in this world; they are scenes that no one can truly know until they see them.

And since the announcement of the occurrence of these terrifying events on the scene of Resurrection—or prior to it—is a warning to all criminals and believers, and is a grace among the Divine graces, He repeats the same previous sentence afterward, saying: "Then which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

In the next verse, moving from the cosmic events of Resurrection to the state of the sinful human on that day, It adds: "On that Day, no human or jinn will be questioned about his sin" (فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لا يُسْئَلُ عَنْ ذَنْبِهِ إِنْسٌ وَ لا جَانٌ).

Why do they not question? Because everything is manifest on that day; it is the "Day of Manifestation" (Yawm al-Buruz), and everything can be read in the faces of humans.

It might be imagined that this verse contradicts those verses that explicitly and emphatically mention the questioning of servants on the Day of Resurrection, such as verse 24 of Surah As-Saffat: "And stop them; indeed, they are to be questioned" (وَقِفُوهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ مَسْئُولُونَ), and verses 92-93 of Surah Al-Hijr: "So by your Lord, We will surely question them all about what they used to do" (فَوَرَبِّكَ لَنَسْئَلَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ عَمَّا كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

However, by noting one point, this problem is resolved: Resurrection is a very long day, and man must pass through numerous stations and crossings, standing for a duration at each scene and station. According to some narrations, there are fifty such stations. In some of these stations, absolutely no questions are asked; rather, "the color of the face reveals the secrets within," as will be mentioned in the following verses.

In some stations, a seal is placed upon the human's mouth and the limbs of the body rise to testify [^1]. In others, humans are questioned precisely [^2], and in yet others, humans rise to argue, defend, and litigate [^3]. In short, each scene has its own conditions, and each scene is more fearsome than the last.

And again, following that, addressing everyone, He says: "Then which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

Yes, on that day they are not questioned; rather, "The criminals will be known by their marks" (يُعْرَفُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ بِسِيماهُمْ) [^1]. One group will have cheerful, luminous, and radiant faces, expressing their faith and righteous deeds, while another group will have black, dark, ugly, and scowling faces, which are signs of their disbelief and sin, as we read in verses 38 to 41 of Surah Abasa: "[Some] faces, that Day, will be bright—laughing, rejoicing at good news. And [other] faces, that Day, will have upon them dust. Blackness will cover them."

Then It adds: "Then they will be seized by the forelocks and the feet [and cast into Hell]!" (فَيُؤْخَذُ بِالنَّواصِي وَ الْأَقْدامِ).

"Nawasi" (نَواصِي) is the plural of "Nasiyah" (ناحية). As Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: it originally means the hair at the front of the head and is derived from the root "Nasa" (نصا) meaning connection and continuity. "Seizing by the forelock" means grabbing the hair at the front of the head, and it is sometimes used as a metaphor for complete dominance over something.

"Aqdām" (أَقْدام) is the plural of "Qadam," meaning feet.

Seizing the criminals by the forelocks and the feet may be meant in its literal sense—that the angels of punishment grab these two and lift them off the ground, casting them into Hell with ultimate humiliation—or it may be a metaphor for their extreme weakness and helplessness in the grip of the agents of Divine punishment, who take this group to Hell with total ignominy. And what a painful and terrifying scene that is!

Again, since reminding of these matters regarding the Hereafter is a warning and a grace to everyone, He adds: "Then which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

In the next verse, He says: "This is the Hell which the criminals used to deny" (هذِهِ جَهَنَّمُ الَّتِي يُكَذِّبُ بِهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ). Regarding whether the addressees are those present at the Gathering and this speech is said to them on the Day of Resurrection, or if the addressee is the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself and it was said to him in this world, commentators have various interpretations. However, there are clues in the verse that strengthen the second meaning, because the use of the present tense verb (yukadhdhibu - deny) and the use of the third-person plural "the criminals" indicates that Allah is saying to His Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

These are the descriptions of the Hell that the criminals constantly deny in this world. Or, the addressees are all humans and jinn, whom He warns that the Hell denied by the criminals has such qualities as you hear; you must be vigilant so that your end does not lead there.

Again, in describing Hell and its painful punishments, He adds: "The criminals will go around between it and scalding water, heated to the utmost degree" (يَطُوفُونَ بَيْنَها وَ بَيْنَ حَمِيمٍ آنٍ).

"An" (آنٍ) and "Ani" (آنى) here mean water that is at the ultimate level of heat and burning. It is originally derived from the root "Ana" (انا) meaning "time," because the scalding water has reached its final time and stage [of boiling].

In this way, on one hand, they burn amidst the scorching flames of Hell, becoming thirsty and longing for water, and on the other hand, they are given boiling water (or it is poured upon them), and this is a painful punishment.

From some verses of the Quran, it is understood that the scalding spring of Hamim is beside Hell; the inhabitants of Hell are first taken into it and then cast into the fire of Hell:

"They will be dragged into the scalding water; then in the Fire they will be burned" (Surah Al-Mu'min, 71-72).

The expression "going around between it and scalding water" in the verse under discussion is also consistent with this meaning.

Again, following this severe and awakening warning, which is a grace from Allah's side, He says: "Then which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

[^1]: In this sentence, whether "idha" (إِذَا) is conditional, sudden, or temporal has several possibilities, but the first is best, and the result of the condition is omitted. It may be estimated as: "When the heaven is rent asunder and becomes rose-red like molten oil, there will be horrors that description cannot endure." [^1]: Surah Ya-Sin, 65. [^2]: Like the verse under discussion and the two verses mentioned above. [^3]: Surah Al-Nahl, 111. [^1]: "Sima" (سيما) originally means "sign or mark," and includes any kind of sign or mark that may be on the face or other parts of their bodies. However, since signs of happiness or misery appear mostly on the face, the face is usually evoked when this word is mentioned.

Ayahs

وَلِمَنْ خَافَ مَقَامَ رَبِّهِۦ جَنَّتَانِ (٤٦)
For him who stands in awe of his Lord will be two gardens. (46)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٧)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (47)
ذَوَاتَآ أَفْنَانٍ (٤٨)
Both abounding in branches. (48)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٩)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (49)
فِيهِمَا عَيْنَانِ تَجْرِيَانِ (٥٠)
In both of them will be two flowing springs. (50)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٥١)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (51)
فِيهِمَا مِن كُلِّ فَـٰكِهَةٍ زَوْجَانِ (٥٢)
In both of them will be two kinds of every fruit. (52)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٥٣)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (53)
مُتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَىٰ فُرُشٍۭ بَطَآئِنُهَا مِنْ إِسْتَبْرَقٍ ۚ وَجَنَى ٱلْجَنَّتَيْنِ دَانٍ (٥٤)
[They will be] reclining on beds lined with green silk. And the fruit of the two gardens will be near at hand. (54)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٥٥)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (55)

Commentary

These Two Gardens Await Those Who Fear

In these verses, the Quran leaves the people of Hell to their fate and turns toward the inhabitants of Paradise, enumerating portions of the delightful, peerless, and soul-stirring blessings of Heaven, so that by comparison with the severe and painful punishments of the dwellers of Hell, the significance of each becomes clearer.

It says: "But for him who fears the standing before his Lord, there are two gardens" (وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ).

"Fear of the standing before the Lord" either means fear of the stations of the Day of Resurrection and standing before Him for the reckoning, or it means fear of God’s divine knowledge and His constant surveillance over all human beings. ^1

^1: In the first case, Maqam is a noun of place, and in the second case, it is a mim-infinitive (masdar mimi).

The second interpretation is consistent with what appears in Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 33, which says: أَفَمَنْ هُوَ قائِمٌ عَلى كُلِّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ: "Is He then who stands over every soul [watching] what it has earned [like any other]?"

And in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read regarding the interpretation of this verse: "Whoever knows that Allah sees him, hears what he says, and knows what he does of good or evil, and this prevents him from ugly deeds, he is the one who fears the standing before his Lord and restrains his soul from [low] desires." ^1

Here, there is a third interpretation: that the intent is fear of God not because of the fire of Hell or greed for the delights of Paradise, but solely out of fear of the "Station of the Lord and His Majesty."

Furthermore, a fourth interpretation exists: that "the standing before the Lord" refers to the station of His Justice, for His Holy Essence is not a cause for fear; rather, the fear is of His Justice, and fear of Justice ultimately returns to fear of one's own actions. For he who is pure, what has he to fear from the reckoning? Criminals are afraid when they pass by a courthouse or a prison, but for the innocent, it makes no difference whether that place is a court or anywhere else.

^1: Usul al-Kafi, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 197. From the end of the hadith, it appears the Imam spoke these words in interpretation of the verse "وَأَمَّا مَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ وَنَهَى النَّفْسَ عَنِ الْهَوى" (Surah An-Nazi'at: 40), although the content of both verses is the same.

Of course, fear of the Lord has various sources: sometimes it is those very impure deeds and polluted thoughts; sometimes for those drawn near (Muqarrabun), the slightest "leaving of the better option" (tark al-awla) or negligence is a cause for their dread; and sometimes, without any of these, when they contemplate that Infinite Essence and Boundless Grandeur, they feel insignificance before Him and a state of fear overcomes them. This is a fear that results from the ultimate gnosis (ma'rifah) of the Lord and is specific to the gnostics and the sincere ones of His threshold.

These four interpretations do not contradict one another and may be combined within the meaning of the verse.

As for "Jannatan" (two gardens), it is possible that the first garden is a material and physical paradise, and the second is a spiritual and internal paradise, as mentioned in Surah Al-Imran, verse 15: "For those who fear Allah, there are gardens with their Lord beneath which rivers flow, wherein they abide eternally, and purified spouses and approval from Allah."

In this verse, in addition to the physical paradise where rivers flow beneath trees and pure spouses reside, it speaks of a spiritual paradise which is "the approval (Ridwan) from Allah."

Or, the first garden is given to them as a reward for their deeds, and the second as a grace (fadl), as stated in Surah An-Nur, verse 38: لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنَ ما عَمِلُوا وَيَزِيدَهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ: "That Allah may reward them for the best of what they did and increase them from His grace."

Or, one garden is for obedience, and the other for abstaining from sin.

Or, one is for faith and belief, and the other for righteous deeds and the like.

Or, since the addressees are Jinn and Men, each of these two gardens belongs to one of these groups.

Of course, none of these interpretations has a specific exclusive proof, yet all of them may be combined in the verse. What is certain is that Allah places multiple gardens of Paradise at the disposal of His righteous servants in which they move about; while the dwellers of Hell "circulate between fire and scalding water," these ones are in the gardens of Paradise.

Following this great blessing, He addresses everyone, saying: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

Then, in describing these two gardens, He adds: "Having spreading branches [of various kinds]" (ذَواتا أَفْنانٍ). "Dhawata" is the dual form of "Dhat," meaning "possessor" or "owner." ^1 "Afnan" is the plural of "Fanan" (rhyming with qalam), which originally means fresh, leafy branches, and is sometimes used to mean "type" or "kind." In the verse under discussion, it could be used in either of these two meanings. In the first case, it refers to the fresh, vibrant branches of the heavenly trees, unlike the trees of this world which have old, young, and withered branches.

In the second case, it refers to the diversity of the blessings of Paradise and its various types of bounties.

^1: According to some, the origin of "Dhat," which is singular feminine, was "Dhawat," where the 'w' was omitted for ease of pronunciation, becoming "Dhat." Since the dual form returns words to their original form, it became "Dhawatan" here, and the 'n' was dropped during annexation (idafah). In Majma' al-Bahrayn, it is stated that the root of "Dhu" was "Dhawa" (rhyming with 'asa), thus it is not surprising that its feminine is "Dhawat."

Using it in both meanings is also permissible. There is also the possibility that the heavenly trees are such that a single tree has different branches, and on each branch is a different kind of fruit.

Following this blessing, the same question is repeated: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

Since a lush, green, and vibrant garden must have flowing springs of water in addition to trees, He adds in the next verse: "In both of them are two springs, flowing" (فِيهِما عَيْنانِ تَجْرِيانِ).

Again, in the face of this blessing, the same question is posed: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ). Although the above verse does not mention the nature of these two springs—only using an indefinite form (nakirah), which in such cases indicates greatness and importance—some commentators have interpreted these two springs as the springs of "Salsabil" and "Tasnim," mentioned respectively in Surah Ad-Dahr (76:18) and Surah Al-Mutaffifin (83:27). Sometimes it is said that one of these two is a spring of "pure wine" (sharabun tahur) and the second is a spring of "clarified honey," both of which are mentioned in Surah Muhammad, verse 15.

And if we interpret "Jannatan" in the previous verses as spiritual and material paradises, then naturally these two springs would also be one spiritual (like the spring of gnosis) and the other material (like springs of crystal-clear water, or milk, honey, and pure wine). However, there is no specific proof for any of these interpretations.

In the next verse, when it is the turn of the fruits of these two heavenly gardens, He says: "In both of them, of every fruit, are two kinds" (فِيهِما مِنْ كُلِّ فاكِهَةٍ زَوْجانِ).

One kind of which you have seen an example in the world, and another kind the like of which you have never seen in this world.

Some commentators have also interpreted it as two types: summer and winter, or dry and fresh, or small and large; but none of these has a clear proof. This much is certain: the fruits of Paradise are completely diverse and varied, and each is better and more attractive than the other.

He adds again: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

In the preceding verses, three aspects of the characteristics of these two heavenly gardens were discussed. Now we move to the fourth characteristic, where He says: "[They are] reclining on beds whose linings are of silk brocade" (مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلى فُرُشٍ بَطائِنُها مِنْ إِسْتَبْرَقٍ). ^1 Usually, a person reclines when they are in a calm environment and in ultimate security; this expression is a sign of the complete peace of soul of the inhabitants of Paradise. "Furush" (rhyming with shutur) is the plural of "Firash," meaning carpets or bedding that is spread out.

"Bata'in" is the plural of "Bitanah," meaning lining, and "Istabraq" means thick silk.

It is noteworthy that here, the most expensive fabric imaginable in the world is mentioned as the lining of these carpets, indicating that the outer part is something whose softness, beauty, and attractiveness are beyond description. For usually in this world, the lining is made of less important material because it is not visible; thus, the least important materials of that world are the most valuable materials of this world. Now one must consider what the high-value goods of that place are like.

What is certain is that the divine bounties in the hereafter are not something that can be contained in our words, or even that we have the power to imagine; only a distant shadow manifests for us through these meaningful verses.

It is also noteworthy that in other verses of the Quran, we read that the inhabitants of Paradise recline on "Ara'ik" (canopied couches) and "Surur" (thrones without canopies), but here it says they recline on carpets. This may all be for the sake of variety in heavenly pleasures—sometimes reclining on one and sometimes on the other—and it is also possible that these precious carpets are spread upon those thrones, or it refers to more important matters that are impossible for us, the inhabitants of this world, to perceive.

Finally, in the fifth blessing, He refers to the accessibility of the bounties of this heavenly garden, saying: "And the fruit of both gardens is hanging low" (وَجَنَى الْجَنَّتَيْنِ دانٍ).

Yes, the hardship that usually exists in picking the fruits of the world is not there at all. "Jana" (rhyming with baqa) means fruit that has reached the time for picking, and "Dan" is originally "Dani," meaning near.

And again, He addresses everyone here, saying: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

Ayahs

فِيهِنَّ قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلَا جَآنٌّ (٥٦)
In them are maidens of restrained glances, whom no human has touched before, nor jinn. (56)

So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (57)
كَأَنَّهُنَّ ٱلْيَاقُوتُ وَٱلْمَرْجَانُ (٥٨)
As though they were rubies and corals. (58)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٥٩)

هَلْ جَزَآءُ ٱلْإِحْسَـٰنِ إِلَّا ٱلْإِحْسَـٰنُ (٦٠)

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦١)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (61)

Commentary

Beautiful Heavenly Spouses

In the preceding verses, five types of blessings and characteristics of these two gardens of Paradise were mentioned. Here, we turn to the sixth blessing, which is the pure heavenly spouses. It says: "In these heavenly palaces are women who limit their gaze only to their husbands, and love none but them" (فِيهِنَّ قاصِراتُ الطَّرْفِ) [1] "and no human or jinn has touched them before them" (لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنْسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَ لا جَانٌّ) [2]. Therefore, they are virgins, untouched, and pure in every respect.

It is narrated from Abu Dharr that "the heavenly spouse says to her husband: 'By the glory of my Lord, I find nothing in Paradise better than you; praise be to Allah who made me your spouse and made you mine.'" [3] The word tarf (rhyming with harf) means the eyelid, and since the eyelids move during looking, it is an allusion to gazing. Therefore, the expression qasirat al-tarf refers to women who have a "shortened gaze," meaning they reserve their love and gaze only for their husbands. This is one of the greatest merits of a spouse—to think of no one but her husband and to have affection for no one else.

[1]: The plural pronoun in fihinna (in them) may refer to the "heavenly palaces," the "various gardens of those two Paradises," or "their blessings and bounties."

[2]: Lam yatmithhunna is derived from tamth, which originally refers to menstrual blood and has come to mean the loss of virginity. Here, it indicates that the virginal women of Paradise have never had previous spouses.

[3]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 208.

Following this heavenly blessing, He repeats: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

Then, providing further description of these heavenly spouses, He says: "They are as if they were rubies and coral!" (كَأَنَّهُنَّ الْياقُوتُ وَ الْمَرْجانُ).

In the redness, clarity, and brilliance of "rubies" (yaqut) and the whiteness and beauty of "coral" (marjan) branches; when these two colors (transparent red and white) blend, they produce the most beautiful hue.

"Yaqut" is a mineral stone, usually red, and "marjan" is a marine animal resembling tree branches that is sometimes white, sometimes red, or other colors. Here, it apparently refers to the white variety. [1]

Once again, following this heavenly blessing, He says: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

At the conclusion of this discussion, He says: "Is the reward for goodness [anything] but goodness?" (هَلْ جَزاءُ الْإِحْسانِ إِلَّا الْإِحْسانُ) [2]. Can those who did good deeds in the world expect anything other than the good divine reward? Although in Islamic narrations or the commentaries of exegetes, "goodness" (ihsan) in this verse has been interpreted specifically as "Monotheism" (Tawhid), or "Monotheism and Knowledge," or "Islam," it is evident that each of these is a clear manifestation of this broad concept that encompasses every kind of goodness in belief, speech, and action.

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "There is a verse in the Book of Allah that is universal." I asked, "Which one is it?" He said: "The words of Allah, the Mighty and Sublime: 'Is the reward for goodness [anything] but goodness?' It applies to the disbeliever and the believer, the righteous and the wicked. Whoever has a favor done for him must reciprocate it, and reciprocation is not merely doing as he did, but rather doing more; for if you only do as he did, the merit remains with him for having initiated it!" [1] Therefore, the divine reward in the Hereafter will also be greater than human actions in this world, according to the same reasoning the Imam provided in the aforementioned hadith.

Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Ihsan is something superior to justice ('adl), because justice is for a person to give what is due from him and take what belongs to him, but ihsan is to do more than what is his duty and take less than what is his right."

[1]: We have provided more details regarding coral (marjan) at the beginning of this Surah under the commentary of verse 22.

[2]: The word hal in this verse is for rhetorical negation (istifham inkari). In reality, this verse serves as a proof for the preceding verses that spoke of the six blessings of the people of Paradise.

[1]: Tafsir al-Ayyashi (as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 199, and Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 208).

Once again, He seeks acknowledgment from His servants: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

This is because this law—the reward of goodness with goodness—is itself a great blessing from the Great Lord. It shows that His reward for the actions of His servants is commensurate with His generosity, not the weight of their deeds. Furthermore, if they perform any deed or obedience, it is by the success (tawfiq) and grace of Allah, and its blessings return to themselves.

Key Point: Goodness is the Reward for Goodness

What we read in the above verse, "Is the reward for goodness [anything] but goodness?", is a general law in the logic of the Quran that encompasses God, the creation, and all servants.

The universality of this law teaches all Muslims to reciprocate every act of goodness, regardless of who performed it. As Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said, reciprocation is not merely doing the same, but it should be superior; otherwise, the one who initiated the goodness retains the superiority! Regarding our deeds before Allah, the matter takes another form, for the other party is the Generous Lord whose waves of mercy have encompassed the entire universe. His honor and bounty are worthy of His Essence, not equal to the weight of the servants' deeds. Therefore, it is not surprising that we repeatedly read in the history of nations that individuals, by performing a small act out of sincerity, became recipients of great favors. Among these:

Some commentators have narrated that "one of the Muslims saw an old disbelieving woman scattering seeds for birds during the winter. He said to her, 'Such an act will not be accepted from the likes of you.' She replied, 'I do this whether it is accepted or not.' Time passed, and later he saw her in the precinct of the Kaaba. She said: 'O man, Allah bestowed the blessing of Islam upon me by the grace of a handful of seeds for the birds!'" [1]

[1]: Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 310.

Ayahs

وَمِن دُونِهِمَا جَنَّتَانِ (٦٢)
Beside these two, there will be two [other] gardens. (62)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦٣)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (63)
مُدْهَآمَّتَانِ (٦٤)
Dark green. (64)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦٥)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (65)
فِيهِمَا عَيْنَانِ نَضَّاخَتَانِ (٦٦)
In both of them will be two gushing springs. (66)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦٧)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (67)
فِيهِمَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ وَنَخْلٌ وَرُمَّانٌ (٦٨)
In both of them will be fruits, date-palms and pomegranates. (68)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦٩)
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (69)

Commentary

Two Other Paradises with Their Wondrous Attributes

In continuation of the previous discussion which spoke about the two Paradises for those who fear Allah with their sublime characteristics, these verses speak of two other Paradises that are at a lower level and are naturally for individuals who stand at a lower level of faith and fear of the Lord. In other words, the aim is to describe the existence of a hierarchy corresponding to faith and righteous deeds.

First, He says: "And besides them are two other gardens" (وَ مِنْ دُونِهِما جَنَّتانِ‌).

Two interpretations have been mentioned for this phrase. The first is what we mentioned above. The second interpretation is that the expression "min dunihima" means "besides those two Paradises," implying two other Paradises for the same believers to move between these heavenly gardens for the sake of variety, as human nature seeks variety and finds pleasure in it.

However, considering the tone of these verses, as well as the narrations transmitted regarding its interpretation, the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

In a tradition from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that in interpreting this verse he said:

جنتان من فضة، آنيتهما و ما فيهما، جنتان من ذهب آنيتهما و ما فيهما:

"Two Paradises whose structures and everything within them are of silver, and two Paradises whose structures and everything within them are of gold." (The reference to gold and silver may indicate the difference in the value of their blessings) [^1]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses being discussed.

In another tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that in interpreting this same verse he said:

لا تقولن الجنة واحدة، ان اللَّه يقول:"وَ مِنْ دُونِهِما جَنَّتانِ‌" و لا تقولن درجة واحدة ان اللَّه يقول:" درجات بعضها فوق بعض" انما تفاضل القوم بالاعمال:

"Do not say that Paradise is one, for Allah says: 'And besides them are two other gardens'; and do not say it is a single degree, for Allah says: 'Degrees, some of them above others.' Indeed, the superiority of the people is due to their deeds." [^1] Based on this same principle, it is narrated in a tradition from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

جنتان من ذهب للمقربين، و جنتان من ورق لاصحاب اليمين:

"Two Paradises of gold for the Foremost (al-Muqarrabun), and two Paradises of silver for the Companions of the Right (Ashab al-Yamin)." [^2]

Then He adds: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ‌).

Next, He proceeds to five characteristics of these two Paradises, some of which are similar to what was said about the previous two Paradises and some of which are different, saying: "Both are dark green [with foliage]" (مُدْهامَّتانِ‌). "Mudhammatan" is from the root "idhimam" and the origin "duhmah" (on the pattern of tuhmah), which originally means "blackness" and "the darkness of the night." Subsequently, it has been applied to a very deep green color; and since such a color is a sign of the ultimate freshness and lushness of plants and trees, this expression describes the peak of the verdancy of those two Paradises.

Again, He adds here: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ‌).

In the next verse, He provides another description, saying: "In both of them are two springs gushing forth" (فِيهِما عَيْنانِ نَضَّاخَتانِ‌). "Nadhakhatan" is from the root "nadhkh," meaning the gushing or spurting of water.

Once more, He asks the jinn and humans in the form of a rhetorical question: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ‌).

The next verse says regarding the fruits of these two Paradises: "In both of them are fruit and palm trees and pomegranates" (فِيهِما فاكِهَةٌ وَ نَخْلٌ وَ رُمَّانٌ‌). Undoubtedly, "fakiha" (fruit) has a broad meaning and includes all types of fruit, but the importance of dates and pomegranates has caused them to be mentioned specifically by name. The notion held by some commentators that these two fruits are not included in the concept of "fakiha" is a mistake, as linguists have rejected this; fundamentally, the conjunction of the "specific" to the "general" is quite common in cases where a distinction exists, as we read in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 98: مَنْ كانَ عَدُوًّا لِلَّهِ وَ مَلائِكَتِهِ وَ رُسُلِهِ وَ جِبْرِيلَ وَ مِيكالَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ عَدُوٌّ لِلْكافِرِينَ‌: "Whoever is an enemy to Allah and His angels and His messengers and Gabriel and Michael (is a disbeliever and) Allah is an enemy to the disbelievers."

Here, "Gabriel" and "Michael," who are two of Allah's great angels, are specifically noted after the general mention of "angels."

He repeats the same question again, saying: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ‌).

Key Point: The Value of Fruits

It is noteworthy that in the above verses, among heavenly foods, emphasis is placed specifically on fruits, and among all fruits, dates and pomegranates are mentioned by name. Interestingly, instead of "dates," the "palm tree" (nakhl) is mentioned, but in the case of the pomegranate, the name of the fruit itself is used; surely each has a subtle point.

The specific mention of fruits among heavenly foods is due to the importance fruits have in nutrition, to the extent that humans have been called "frugivorous" (fruit-eating) beings. The role of fruits in human freshness, vitality, and joy is evident not only scientifically but also through the general experience of people.

As for mentioning the palm tree instead of its fruit, it may be because the palm tree, in addition to its fruit, is utilized in various ways, whereas the pomegranate tree is not. From palm leaves, various life necessities are woven—from carpets to hats, clothing, means of transport, and beds—and its fibers are used for various purposes. Some of its parts have medicinal properties, and even its trunk is used as pillars for some buildings or bridges for crossing streams.

The selection of these two fruits from among heavenly fruits is due to their diversity: one often grows in hot regions and the other in cold regions; one contains sugary substances and the other acidic substances; one is hot in nature and the other is cold in nature; one is a food and the other is a quencher of thirst.

Regarding the vital substances present in dates and its types of vitamins discovered today—amounting to 13 vital substances and 5 types of vitamins—as well as its other properties, we have discussed this under verse 25 of Surah Maryam under the title of a "strengthening food." [^1] As for the "pomegranate," which is introduced in some Islamic narrations as the best of fruits (Sayyid al-Fakiha), [^2] nutritionists have said much, including emphasizing its power to purify the blood and the presence of a significant amount of "Vitamin C" in it.

Many other benefits for the pomegranate (sweet and sour) are mentioned in books, including strengthening the stomach, repelling bilious fevers, jaundice, scabies (itching disease), strengthening eyesight, healing chronic wounds, strengthening gums, and treating diarrhea.

In a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

اطعموا صبيانكم الرمان، فانه اسرع لشبابهم:

"Feed your children pomegranates, for it causes them to grow faster." [^3] And in another tradition it is said:

فانه اسرع لالسنتهم:

"They will begin to speak sooner." [^4]

[^1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 13, p. 46. [^2]: This expression is narrated in a tradition from the Noble Prophet of Islam (بحار الانوار جلد 66 صفحه 163). [^3]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 66, p. 164. [^4]: Ibid, p. 165.

In another tradition from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليهما السلام, it is narrated that they said:

ما على وجه الارض ثمرة كانت احب الى رسول اللَّه (ص) من الرمان:

"There was no fruit on the face of the earth more beloved to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله than the pomegranate." [^1]

[^1]: Al-Kafi, Vol. 6, p. 352.

Blessed is the Name of your Lord, the Majestic and the Munificent! (78)

Commentary

Once Again, the Heavenly Spouses

In continuation of the description of the blessings of the two Paradises mentioned in the previous verses, these verses point to other aspects of these bounties.

First, It says: "In them are women of good character and beautiful faces" (فِيهِنَّ خَيْراتٌ حِسانٌ) [^1]. These are women who combine "beauty of character" with "beauty of form," for the word khayr (good) is mostly used regarding virtuous traits and spiritual beauty, while husn (beauty) is mostly used for outward physical beauty and charm.

In the narrations regarding the interpretation of this verse, many noble qualities are listed for the heavenly spouses, which can also serve as an indication of the high qualities of women in this world and a model for all women; including being pleasant of speech, cleanliness and purity, not causing harm, not looking at strangers, and the like.

[^1]: The pronoun fihinna, which is feminine plural, may refer to the total of the four Paradises mentioned in the previous verses, or it may refer to the two Paradises recently mentioned, considering the numerous gardens and various palaces they contain; this latter interpretation is more appropriate as it keeps their accounts separate.

In short, all the qualities of perfection and beauty desired in a spouse are gathered in them; they possess all that the virtuous possess collectively. For this reason, the Quran has described them with a short and profound expression as khayratun hisan. [^1]

Following the mention of this blessing, He adds: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

Then, in continuing the description of these heavenly women, He adds: "They are Houris, secluded in pavilions" (حُورٌ مَقْصُوراتٌ فِي الْخِيامِ). Hur is the plural of Hawra’ and Ahwar, referring to someone whose pupils are intensely black and whose whites of the eyes are intensely transparent and bright; sometimes it is also applied to fair-skinned women.

The expression maqsurat (secluded/restricted) indicates that they belong only to their spouses and are veiled and hidden from others.

Khiyam is the plural of khaymah (tent/pavilion). However, as stated in Islamic narrations, the pavilions of Paradise bear no resemblance to the tents of this world in terms of their vastness, extent, and beauty.

It is also noteworthy that khaymah, according to what linguists and some...

[^1]: Some have said khayrat is the plural of khayrah (on the pattern of sayyidah), which was shortened to khayrat, and some consider it the plural of khayrah (on the pattern of hayrah). In any case, it has a descriptive meaning and is not a superlative (af'al al-tafdil), because the superlative is not pluralized in this manner.

...commentators have stated, does not only mean the cloth tents known among us; rather, the word khaymah is applied to wooden houses or even any circular house. Sometimes it is said that a khaymah refers to any house that is not built of stone and the like. [^1]

And once again, He repeats the same meaningful question: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

The next verse is another description of the heavenly Houris, stating: "Untouched before them by any man or jinn" (لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنْسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَ لا جَانٌ) [^2]. Of course, as understood from other verses of the Quran, men and women who were spouses in this world, if both are believers and people of Paradise, will be joined together there and live together in the best conditions and states [^3]. It is even understood from narrations that the status of these [earthly] women is superior to the Houris of Paradise [^4] because of the worship and righteous deeds they performed in this world.

Then He says: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ).

[^1]: Lisan al-Arab, Majma' al-Bahrayn, and Al-Munjid.

[^2]: Regarding the meaning of tamt, we provided sufficient explanation under verse 56 of this same Surah.

[^3]: (Ar-Ra'd: 23 and Ghafir: 8).

[^4]: Al-Durr al-Manthur, vol. 6, p. 151.

In the final description of the heavenly blessings in these verses, He says: "The inhabitants of these two Paradises are reclining on green cushions and rich, beautiful carpets" (مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلى‌ رَفْرَفٍ خُضْرٍ وَ عَبْقَرِيٍّ حِسانٍ).

Rafraf originally means the spreading leaves of trees, and was later applied to beautiful, colorful fabrics that resemble the scenery of gardens [^1]. Abqari originally means any unique or rare being; hence, scholars whose presence among people is rare are called abaqirah. Many believe the word Abqar was initially a name the Arabs chose for a city of jinn, and since that city was an unknown and rare subject, they attributed any unique thing to it and called it abqari. Some have also said that Abqar was a city where the finest silk fabrics were woven [^2]. In any case, the original root is now obsolete, and abqari is used as an independent word meaning "rarely found" or "precious," and although it is singular, it sometimes conveys a plural meaning (as in the verse under discussion).

And hisan is the plural of hasan (on the pattern of chaman), meaning good and beautiful.

In any case, these expressions all indicate that everything in Paradise is distinguished: the fruits, the food, the palaces, the carpets—in short, everything is unique and peerless in its kind. Rather, it must be said that even these expressions can never...

[^1]: Some commentators have mentioned the famous "Negarestan" carpet of Anushirvan as an example here—that exceptionally expensive carpet that reflected the view of a garden.

[^2]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, under the verses in question.

...contain those great and peerless concepts; they only draw a shadow of it in our minds.

Then, for the last time (and the thirty-first time), He asks this question of jinn and men and all individuals: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ). Spiritual blessings? Or material blessings? Blessings of this world? Or blessings of Paradise?

Blessings that encompass your entire being and in which you are constantly immersed, yet sometimes due to pride and negligence, you consign them all to oblivion and become heedless of the Giver of all these blessings and the One whose future blessings you await? Which one do you deny?

And in the final verse of this Surah, He says: "Blessed and everlasting is the name of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor" (تَبارَكَ اسْمُ رَبِّكَ ذِي الْجَلالِ وَ الْإِكْرامِ). Tabaraka is from the root baraka (on the pattern of darak), meaning the chest of a camel. Since camels press their chests to the ground when they lie down and remain fixed in a place, this word is used to mean remaining fixed, stable, and enduring. Also, since an enduring and imperishable capital yields many benefits, a highly beneficial being is called mubarak (blessed). The most deserving being for this title is the Holy Essence of Allah, who is the source of all goodness and blessings.

This expression is used because this Surah mentions various types of divine blessings—in the earth and the heavens, in the creation of man, and in this world and the hereafter. Since all of these...

...are emanated from the blessed existence of the Lord, the most appropriate expression is that which appears in this verse.

For "Name" here refers to the attributes of the Lord, especially the attribute of "Mercy" (Rahmaniyyah) which is the source of all these blessings. In other words, the actions of Allah stem from His attributes. If He created the universe with order and placed a balance in everything, His "Wisdom" necessitated it. If He made the law of justice prevail in everything, His "Knowledge and Justice" necessitated it. If He overtakes criminals with the various punishments mentioned in this Surah, His being the "Avenger" requires it. And if He encompasses the righteous believers with various spiritual and material bounties in this world and the next, His vast "Grace and Mercy" necessitate it. Therefore, His "Name" refers to His "Attributes," and His attributes are identical to His Holy Essence.

The expression Dhi al-Jalal wa al-Ikram (Owner of Majesty and Honor) is an allusion to all His attributes of Beauty and Majesty (Dhu al-Jalal refers to the negative attributes [attributes of majesty/exemption], and Dhu al-Ikram refers to the positive attributes [attributes of beauty/perfection]).

It is interesting that this Surah began with the name of Allah as al-Rahman (The Most Merciful) and ends with the name of the Lord as Dhu al-Jalal wa al-Ikram, and both are harmonious with the entire content of the Surah.

Key Points

1- In verse 37 of this Surah, after mentioning various spiritual and material blessings of the world, He says: "And there will remain the Face of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor" (وَ يَبْقى‌ وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ذُو الْجَلالِ وَ الْإِكْرامِ). At the end of the Surah, after mentioning various heavenly blessings, He says: "Blessed is the name of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor" (تَبارَكَ اسْمُ رَبِّكَ ذِي الْجَلالِ وَ الْإِكْرامِ).

These two expressions reflect the reality that all lines lead back to His Holy Essence, and whatever exists is from His side; the world is from Him, and the hereafter is also from Him, and His Majesty and Honor encompass everything.

2- In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: A person was supplicating in his presence, saying: "O Owner of Majesty and Honor" (Ya Dha al-Jalal wa al-Ikram). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Your prayer has been answered, so ask [whatever you wish]!" [^1] In another hadith, it is mentioned that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saw a man standing and engaged in prayer. After the bowing, prostration, and testimony, he supplicated and said in his prayer: "O Allah, I ask You, for to You belongs all praise; there is no god but You, alone, without partner, the Bestower, Originator of the heavens and the earth, O Owner of Majesty and Honor, O Ever-Living, O Self-Subsisting, I ask You..."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"He has called upon Allah by His Great Name, which if He is called by it, He answers, and if He is asked by it, He gives." [^2]

3- In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read regarding the interpretation of the verse "Blessed is the name of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor": "We are the Majesty of Allah and His Honor, with which Allah has honored the servants through obedience to us." [^3]

[^1] and [^2]: Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, vol. 6, p. 153.

[^3]: Tafsir al-Burhan, vol. 4, p. 272.

It is clear that the Ahl al-Bayt of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله invite only toward Allah and call only to His obedience; they are the guides of the path and the ships of salvation in this turbulent sea of worldly life. Therefore, they are considered one of the manifestations of Divine Majesty and Honor, as Allah encompasses people with the blessing of guidance through His chosen ones.

4- Some have narrated that the first part of the Holy Quran to be recited openly to the Quraysh in Mecca were the opening verses of this very Surah, "Ar-Rahman." Abdullah ibn Mas'ud says: The companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله gathered and said, "Until now, the Quraysh have heard nothing of the Quran. Who among us will recite it openly to them?" I said, "I am that person!" They said, "We fear they will harm you; it is better that someone undertakes this who has a powerful tribe to defend him." I said, "Leave me be; Allah is my defender."

The next day, at midday, Ibn Mas'ud stood by the Station of Ibrahim (Maqam Ibrahim), and while the Quraysh were sitting in their assembly, he stood up and began with a loud voice:

"In the name of Allah, the Most Merciful, the Most Kind. The Most Merciful taught the Quran..." and he continued. The Quraysh fell silent and listened, then they said, "What is this low-born man saying?!" Some said, "He is reciting part of what Muhammad has brought." They stood up and struck his face hard, but he continued reciting the Surah as far as Allah willed.

Then he returned to the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله while the marks of the wounds were visible on his face. They said, "This is exactly what we feared for you!" Ibn Mas'ud said, "Never have these enemies of Allah been so small in my eyes.

If you wish, I will continue tomorrow as well; I have no fear at all." They said, "No, this much is enough; you have recited to them what they disliked." [^1] For this reason, Ibn Mas'ud is considered the first person to recite the Quran openly to the polytheists in Mecca. [^2]

O Allah! You are the Owner of Majesty and Honor; we swear by Your Majesty and Honor, do not deprive us of the heavenly blessings and bounties. O Lord! The circle of Your mercy is very vast, even though we have not performed deeds worthy of it; treat us as befits Your status of Mercy (Rahmaniyyah).

O God! We do not deny any of Your favors, and we consider ourselves constantly immersed in Your grace; make them enduring for us! Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.
